attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	#!/usr/bin/env bash

3	
4	# onboarding
5	#
6	# First-run briefing for any d* user or their agent. Prints:
7	#   1. Environment: who, what repo, what paths.
8	#   2. Branching policy (summary).
9	#   3. Current repo state: branches, dev-bootstrap status, WIP, pr/ signals.
10	#   4. Commands available: dg-* helpers and AI CLIs.
11	#   5. Concrete next steps.
12	#

14	
15	BOLD=$'\033[1m'; DIM=$'\033[2m'; GREEN=$'\033[32m'; YELLOW=$'\033[33m'; RED=$'\033[31m'; RESET=$'\033[0m'

41	  main          coordinator-only, release branch (tagged)
42	  dev           coordinator-only, integration branch (accumulates merges)
43	  feat/<slug>   you (local), work in progress; base from origin/dev
44	  fix/<slug>    you (local), bug-fix flavor of feat/
45	  pr/<slug>     you (local), signal "ready to merge" (rename feat/ -> pr/)
46	
47	  Rules:
48	    - You never push. The coordinator (mike) relays pr/* into dev and pushes.
49	    - Base new branches on origin/dev, not origin/main.
50	    - No history rewrites on dev or main. Rebase is OK on your own feat/*.
51	    - The ref name is the contract: no PR API, no JSON, no ticketing.
52	
53	  Full policy: /disk1/github/softwarewrighter/devgroup/docs/branching-pr-strategy.md
54	POLICY
55	
56	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

77	  if git -C "$repo_root" rev-parse --verify --quiet origin/dev >/dev/null 2>&1; then

105	    if [[ "$ahead" -gt 0 ]]; then

124	    # Heuristic: any mention of a dg-* helper or the feat/pr/ branch model.
125	    grep -qE 'dg-(env|policy|new-feature|new-fix|mark-pr|list-pr|reap|agentrail-retrofit)|feat/<|pr/<|pr/\*' \
126	      "$claude_md" 2>/dev/null && dg_mentioned=1
127	  fi
128	
129	  # AgentRail state (three cases: saga / pre-retrofit / not used)
130	  if [[ -d "$agentrail_dir" ]]; then
131	    ok "AgentRail saga present — follow the session protocol in CLAUDE.md"
132	  elif (( ar_mentioned )); then
133	    wn "CLAUDE.md references AgentRail but .agentrail/ is missing (pre-retrofit repo)."
134	    v  "      Do NOT run 'agentrail next' here — it will fail on a missing saga."
135	    v  "      To retrofit on a feat branch:  dg-agentrail-retrofit"
136	    v  "      Until then, use plain devgroup branching (feat/ -> pr/)."

149	    v  "      Consider adding one that describes the devgroup branching workflow"
150	    v  "      (feat/<slug> -> dg-mark-pr -> pr/<slug>) and, if applicable, the"

152	  elif (( ! dg_mentioned )); then

154	    v  "      It is stale relative to this host's process. Update it on a feat branch"

156	    v  "      Keep the repo's own project content; only add the missing workflow context."

160	fi

162	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

168	    printf '  %-20s %s\n' "$h_cmd" "(missing)"
169	  fi

177	    printf '  %-10s %s\n' "$tool" "$(command -v "$tool")"
178	  else
179	    printf '  %-10s %s\n' "$tool" "(not installed)"

183	if (( any_missing )); then

188	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

191	  v "  1. cd \$SRCROOT"
192	  v "  2. Re-run: onboarding"
193	elif [[ "$cur_branch" == "dev" ]]; then
194	  v "  • Start a task:   git fetch origin --prune && git switch -c feat/<slug>"

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -Eeuo pipefail

# onboarding
#
# First-run briefing for any d* user or their agent. Prints:
#   1. Environment: who, what repo, what paths.
#   2. Branching policy (summary).
#   3. Current repo state: branches, dev-bootstrap status, WIP, pr/ signals.
#   4. Commands available: dg-* helpers and AI CLIs.
#   5. Concrete next steps.
#
# Invoke at any time, safe to re-run. No side effects.

BOLD=$'\033[1m'; DIM=$'\033[2m'; GREEN=$'\033[32m'; YELLOW=$'\033[33m'; RED=$'\033[31m'; RESET=$'\033[0m'
h()  { printf "\n${BOLD}==> %s${RESET}\n" "$*"; }
k()  { printf "  ${DIM}%s${RESET} " "$1"; }
v()  { printf "%s\n" "$*"; }
ok() { printf "  ${GREEN}%s${RESET} %s\n" "✓" "$*"; }
wn() { printf "  ${YELLOW}%s${RESET} %s\n" "!" "$*"; }
er() { printf "  ${RED}%s${RESET} %s\n" "✗" "$*"; }

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
h "You"
k "user         "; v "${USER}"
k "host         "; v "$(hostname -s)"
k "home         "; v "${HOME}"

h "Paths"
k "DEVWORK      "; v "${DEVWORK:-<unset>}"
k "PRIMARY_REPO "; v "${PRIMARY_REPO:-<unset>}"
k "ORGROOT      "; v "${ORGROOT:-<unset>}"
k "SRCROOT      "; v "${SRCROOT:-<unset>}"
k "REPOROOT     "; v "${REPOROOT:-<unset>}"
k "TOOLROOT     "; v "${TOOLROOT:-<unset>}"
k "SCRIPTROOT   "; v "${SCRIPTROOT:-<unset>}"

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
h "Branching policy (summary)"
cat <<'POLICY'
  main          coordinator-only, release branch (tagged)
  dev           coordinator-only, integration branch (accumulates merges)
  feat/<slug>   you (local), work in progress; base from origin/dev
  fix/<slug>    you (local), bug-fix flavor of feat/
  pr/<slug>     you (local), signal "ready to merge" (rename feat/ -> pr/)

  Rules:
    - You never push. The coordinator (mike) relays pr/* into dev and pushes.
    - Base new branches on origin/dev, not origin/main.
    - No history rewrites on dev or main. Rebase is OK on your own feat/*.
    - The ref name is the contract: no PR API, no JSON, no ticketing.

  Full policy: /disk1/github/softwarewrighter/devgroup/docs/branching-pr-strategy.md
POLICY

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
h "Current repo state"

cwd="$(pwd)"
repo_root=""
if git rev-parse --show-toplevel >/dev/null 2>&1; then
  repo_root="$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)"
fi

if [[ -z "$repo_root" ]]; then
  wn "not inside a git repo. cd \$SRCROOT first."
else
  k "repo         "; v "$repo_root"
  cur_branch="$(git -C "$repo_root" branch --show-current 2>/dev/null || echo '<detached>')"
  k "branch       "; v "$cur_branch"

  # Remotes
  origin_url="$(git -C "$repo_root" remote get-url origin 2>/dev/null || echo '<no origin>')"
  k "origin       "; v "$origin_url"

  # Fetch state (don't actually fetch — just report what we know locally)
  if git -C "$repo_root" rev-parse --verify --quiet origin/dev >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    ok "origin/dev exists — base feat/* on it"
  else
    wn "origin/dev does NOT exist yet. Bootstrap locally:"
    v  "      git switch dev 2>/dev/null || git switch -c dev origin/main"
    v  "    Your local dev will converge once the coordinator creates origin/dev."
  fi

  # Uncommitted changes
  if ! git -C "$repo_root" diff --quiet || ! git -C "$repo_root" diff --cached --quiet; then
    wn "uncommitted changes in worktree:"
    git -C "$repo_root" status --short | sed 's/^/      /'
  else
    ok "worktree clean"
  fi

  # pr/* branches (ready-to-merge signals)
  pr_branches=$(git -C "$repo_root" for-each-ref --format='%(refname:short)' \
                  refs/heads/pr refs/heads/pr/\* 2>/dev/null || true)
  if [[ -n "$pr_branches" ]]; then
    wn "pr/ branches signalling ready (coordinator will relay these):"
    printf '      %s\n' $pr_branches
  fi

  # Unrelayed commits on current feat/fix branch
  if [[ "$cur_branch" == feat/* || "$cur_branch" == fix/* ]] \
     && git -C "$repo_root" rev-parse --verify --quiet origin/dev >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    ahead=$(git -C "$repo_root" rev-list --count origin/dev.."$cur_branch" 2>/dev/null || echo 0)
    if [[ "$ahead" -gt 0 ]]; then
      wn "$cur_branch is $ahead commit(s) ahead of origin/dev (not yet signaled)"
    fi
  fi
fi

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
h "Workflow awareness"

if [[ -z "$repo_root" ]]; then
  wn "not inside a git repo — cannot classify workflow state"
else
  claude_md="$repo_root/CLAUDE.md"
  agentrail_dir="$repo_root/.agentrail"
  ar_cli=0; ar_mentioned=0; dg_mentioned=0; claude_md_exists=0
  command -v agentrail >/dev/null 2>&1 && ar_cli=1
  if [[ -f "$claude_md" ]]; then
    claude_md_exists=1
    grep -qi 'agentrail' "$claude_md" 2>/dev/null && ar_mentioned=1
    # Heuristic: any mention of a dg-* helper or the feat/pr/ branch model.
    grep -qE 'dg-(env|policy|new-feature|new-fix|mark-pr|list-pr|reap|agentrail-retrofit)|feat/<|pr/<|pr/\*' \
      "$claude_md" 2>/dev/null && dg_mentioned=1
  fi

  # AgentRail state (three cases: saga / pre-retrofit / not used)
  if [[ -d "$agentrail_dir" ]]; then
    ok "AgentRail saga present — follow the session protocol in CLAUDE.md"
  elif (( ar_mentioned )); then
    wn "CLAUDE.md references AgentRail but .agentrail/ is missing (pre-retrofit repo)."
    v  "      Do NOT run 'agentrail next' here — it will fail on a missing saga."
    v  "      To retrofit on a feat branch:  dg-agentrail-retrofit"
    v  "      Until then, use plain devgroup branching (feat/ -> pr/)."
  else
    if (( ar_cli )); then
      v "  AgentRail not used in this repo (no .agentrail/, no CLAUDE.md mention)."
      v "  If this repo should adopt AgentRail, retrofit with: dg-agentrail-retrofit"
    else
      v "  AgentRail not installed and not referenced here — use plain devgroup branching."
    fi
  fi

  # CLAUDE.md awareness of devgroup workflow
  if (( ! claude_md_exists )); then
    wn "no CLAUDE.md in this repo."
    v  "      Consider adding one that describes the devgroup branching workflow"
    v  "      (feat/<slug> -> dg-mark-pr -> pr/<slug>) and, if applicable, the"
    v  "      AgentRail session protocol. Do this on a feat branch."
  elif (( ! dg_mentioned )); then
    wn "CLAUDE.md does not mention the devgroup workflow (dg-* helpers or feat/pr/ branches)."
    v  "      It is stale relative to this host's process. Update it on a feat branch"
    v  "      (add a section describing no-push, feat/ -> pr/, coordinator relay)."
    v  "      Keep the repo's own project content; only add the missing workflow context."
  else
    ok "CLAUDE.md mentions the devgroup workflow"
  fi
fi

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
h "Helpers (PATH: \$SCRIPTROOT)"
for h_cmd in dg-env dg-policy dg-new-feature dg-new-fix dg-mark-pr dg-list-pr dg-reap dg-install-agents dg-agentrail-retrofit onboarding; do
  if command -v "$h_cmd" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    printf '  %-20s %s\n' "$h_cmd" "$(command -v "$h_cmd")"
  else
    printf '  %-20s %s\n' "$h_cmd" "(missing)"
  fi
done

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
h "AI CLIs"
any_missing=0
for tool in claude codex gemini opencode; do
  if command -v "$tool" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    printf '  %-10s %s\n' "$tool" "$(command -v "$tool")"
  else
    printf '  %-10s %s\n' "$tool" "(not installed)"
    any_missing=1
  fi
done
if (( any_missing )); then
  v ""
  v "  To install missing CLIs into your home: dg-install-agents"
fi

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
h "Next steps"
if [[ -z "$repo_root" ]]; then
  v "  1. cd \$SRCROOT"
  v "  2. Re-run: onboarding"
elif [[ "$cur_branch" == "dev" ]]; then
  v "  • Start a task:   git fetch origin --prune && git switch -c feat/<slug>"
  v "  • Or a fix:       git switch -c fix/<slug>"
elif [[ "$cur_branch" == feat/* || "$cur_branch" == fix/* ]]; then
  v "  • Keep committing on $cur_branch."
  v "  • When ready:     dg-mark-pr             (renames $cur_branch -> pr/...)"
elif [[ "$cur_branch" == pr/* ]]; then
  v "  • Signaled. The coordinator will relay on next cycle."
  v "  • After merge:    git fetch origin --prune && git switch dev && git branch -D $cur_branch"
else
  v "  • You're on '$cur_branch'. Switch to dev (or create feat/ from origin/dev) to start work."
fi

v ""

lastPrompt: onboarding is in your path

input.command: onboarding
input.description: Run onboarding briefing script

---
name: Never push to remote — coordinator relays
description: Hard rule for this devgroup host: I must never `git push`. The coordinator (mike) relays pr/* branches into dev and pushes.
type: feedback
---

Never run `git push` (or `git push --force`, etc.) in any repo under `$DEVWORK` (`/disk1/github/softwarewrighter/devgroup/work/dcftn`). To signal "ready to merge," rename the local feat/fix branch to `pr/<slug>` (use `dg-mark-pr`). The coordinator relays it.

**Why:** The devgroup model uses *ref names as the contract* — no PR API, no JSON, no ticketing. The coordinator (mike) is the only actor that touches `origin/dev` and `origin/main`. Pushing from a dev account would break the model and likely create conflicting refs.

**How to apply:** Stage and commit freely on `feat/*` / `fix/*`. When the user says "ship it" / "send the PR" / "open a PR," do NOT push or `gh pr create` — instead run `dg-mark-pr` (or `git branch -m feat/x pr/x`). If the user explicitly asks me to push, confirm once and remind them of the policy before doing so.

input.content:
---
name: devgroup branching workflow
description: Branch model used across all devgroup repos: main/dev are coordinator-only; I work on feat/<slug> and signal readiness by renaming to pr/<slug>. Base from origin/dev, not origin/main.
type: project
---

Branch roles:
- `main` — coordinator-only, release branch (tagged)
- `dev` — coordinator-only, integration branch
- `feat/<slug>` — my WIP, base from `origin/dev`
- `fix/<slug>` — bug-fix flavor of `feat/`
- `pr/<slug>` — "ready to merge" signal (rename `feat/` → `pr/`)

Helpers (in `$SCRIPTROOT` = `/disk1/github/softwarewrighter/devgroup/work/scripts`):
- `dg-new-feature <slug>` / `dg-new-fix <slug>` — start a branch from origin/dev
- `dg-mark-pr` — rename current `feat/*` to `pr/*` (the merge signal)
- `dg-list-pr` — list current pr/* branches
- `dg-reap` — clean up after coordinator merges
- `dg-policy` — print full policy summary
- `dg-env` — show resolved devgroup paths
- `dg-agentrail-retrofit` — add AgentRail to a repo on a feat branch
- `onboarding` — re-run the first-run briefing (safe, no side effects)

Rules:
- No history rewrites on `dev` or `main`. Rebase OK on my own `feat/*`.
- The ref name IS the contract — no PR API/JSON/tickets.
- If `origin/dev` doesn't exist yet locally: `git switch dev 2>/dev/null || git switch -c dev origin/main`.

**Why:** The host enforces an asymmetric model where dev accounts (like `dcftn`) cannot push; only the coordinator can. Branch naming is the only signaling mechanism between dev and coordinator.

**How to apply:** Suggest `dg-*` helpers when starting/finishing work rather than raw git incantations. When proposing "open a PR," translate to "run `dg-mark-pr`." When checking what's ready, use `dg-list-pr`.

Full policy doc: `/disk1/github/softwarewrighter/devgroup/docs/branching-pr-strategy.md`

10	## Overview

13

15	
16	- a small, fixed-form, integer-only FORTRAN-like subset
17	- implemented in **SNOBOL4**
18	- targeting **PL/SW**
19	- designed for clarity, testability, and historical flavor rather than full FORTRAN compatibility
20

22	
23	- integer assignment
24	- simple expressions
25	- `GOTO`
26	- counted `DO`
27	- `PRINT`
28	- `READ`
29	- one-dimensional integer arrays
30	
31	The project is intentionally staged:
32	
33	1. normalize and parse fixed-form source
34	2. build symbols and resolve labels
35	3. lower control flow to a simple normalized form
36	4. emit readable PL/SW
37	5. later revisit floating point and richer FORTRAN features

file.content:
# sw-cor24-fortran

Fortran compiler for the COR24 24-bit RISC ISA (future/research).

Part of the [COR24 ecosystem](https://github.com/sw-embed/sw-cor24-project).

**FTI-0**  
*A small educational FORTRAN subset compiler in SNOBOL4 targeting PL/SW.*

## Overview

`sw-cor24-fortran` is an experimental educational compiler project.

The first language subset is **FTI-0**:

- a small, fixed-form, integer-only FORTRAN-like subset
- implemented in **SNOBOL4**
- targeting **PL/SW**
- designed for clarity, testability, and historical flavor rather than full FORTRAN compatibility

The initial goal is to compile small educational programs such as:

- integer assignment
- simple expressions
- `GOTO`
- counted `DO`
- `PRINT`
- `READ`
- one-dimensional integer arrays

The project is intentionally staged:

1. normalize and parse fixed-form source
2. build symbols and resolve labels
3. lower control flow to a simple normalized form
4. emit readable PL/SW
5. later revisit floating point and richer FORTRAN features

## Status

This repository currently defines:

- the project structure
- the FTI-0 language subset
- the compiler architecture
- the PL/SW mapping contract
- the milestone plan

Compiler implementation is expected to begin with source normalization and statement classification.

## Repository structure

- `docs/` — product, architecture, design, plan, subset definition
- `snobol4/` — compiler source and tests
- `examples/` — sample FTI-0 input programs
- `plsw/` — runtime helpers and generated output
- `scripts/` — development helpers

## First milestone

The first executable milestone is:

- accept a tiny `.f` source file
- normalize fixed-form statements
- classify statements
- dump normalized statement records

No code generation is required for the first milestone.

## Naming

- Repository: `sw-cor24-fortran`
- Subset language: `FTI-0`
- Compiler executable: `fti0c`

## Non-goals for FTI-0

FTI-0 does **not** initially support:

- floating point
- `FORMAT`
- `COMMON`
- `EQUIVALENCE`
- statement functions
- separate compilation
- optimization
- multidimensional arrays
- full FORTRAN compatibility

## Long-term direction

Possible later stages include:

- richer FORTRAN subset levels
- arithmetic IF
- subroutines and functions
- software floating point
- direct or indirect lowering into COR24-oriented toolchains

## Research

See `docs/research.txt` for detailed design research and historical context.

## Related Repositories

| Repository | Description |
|---|---|
| [sw-cor24-emulator](https://github.com/sw-embed/sw-cor24-emulator) | COR24 emulator + ISA |
| [sw-cor24-x-assembler](https://github.com/sw-embed/sw-cor24-x-assembler) | COR24 assembler |
| [sw-cor24-project](https://github.com/sw-embed/sw-cor24-project) | Ecosystem hub |

## Links

- Blog: [Software Wrighter Lab](https://software-wrighter-lab.github.io/)
- Discord: [Join the community](https://discord.com/invite/Ctzk5uHggZ)
- YouTube: [Software Wrighter](https://www.youtube.com/@SoftwareWrighter)

## Copyright

Copyright (c) 2026 Michael A. Wright

## License

MIT License. See [LICENSE](LICENSE) for the full text.

1	# FTI-0 Language Subset
2	
3	## Overview
4	
5	FTI-0 is an educational, integer-only, fixed-form subset inspired by classic FORTRAN.
6	
7	It is intentionally smaller and simpler than historical FORTRAN dialects. The goal is to support a teachable compiler pipeline rather than to reproduce every legacy feature or edge case.
8	
9	## Source format
10	
11	FTI-0 source is fixed-form.
12	
13	### Columns
14	
15	- columns 1-5: optional numeric statement label
16	- column 6: continuation marker
17	- columns 7-72: statement body
18	- comment lines begin with `C` or `*` in column 1
19	
20	Anything beyond column 72 is ignored in version 0.
21	
22	## Case
23	
24	The initial implementation may normalize identifiers and keywords to uppercase.
25	
26	## Program structure
27	
28	A small program typically has the form:
29	
30	```fortran
31	      PROGRAM HELLO
32	      INTEGER I
33	      I = 42
34	      PRINT *, I
35	      STOP
36	      END
37	```
38	
39	## Declarations
40	
41	### INTEGER
42	
43	Declares integer scalar variables.
44	
45	Example:
46	
47	```fortran
48	      INTEGER I, N, SUM
49	```
50	
51	### DIMENSION
52	
53	Declares one-dimensional integer arrays.
54	
55	Example:
56	
57	```fortran
58	      DIMENSION A(10)
59	```
60	
61	In FTI-0, arrays are:
62	
63	- integer arrays
64	- one-dimensional only
65	- indexed from 1 to extent
66	
67	## Statements
68	
69	### PROGRAM
70	
71	Defines program name.
72	
73	Example:
74	
75	```fortran
76	      PROGRAM SUM10
77	```
78	
79	### Assignment
80	
81	Example:
82	
83	```fortran
84	      I = 3
85	      SUM = SUM + I
86	      A(I) = I * 10
87	```
88	
89	### GOTO
90	
91	Example:
92	
93	```fortran
94	      GOTO 100
95	```
96	
97	### IF (...) GOTO
98	
99	FTI-0 initially supports the simple form:
100	
101	```fortran
102	      IF (expr) GOTO 100
103	```
104	
105	Semantics:
106	- if `expr` is nonzero, branch to label 100
107	
108	### DO
109	
110	Counted DO with optional default step of 1.
111	
112	Example:
113	
114	```fortran
115	      DO 100 I = 1, 10
116	```
117	
118	A later revision may add explicit step:
119	
120	```fortran

122	```
123	
124	### CONTINUE
125	
126	Example:
127	
128	```fortran
129	  100 CONTINUE
130	```
131	
132	### PRINT
133	
134	Only list-directed style is initially supported.
135	
136	Example:
137	
138	```fortran
139	      PRINT *, I
140	      PRINT *, I, SUM, A(3)
141	```
142	
143	### READ
144	
145	Only list-directed style is initially supported.
146	
147	Example:
148	
149	```fortran
150	      READ *, I, N
151	```
152	
153	### STOP
154	
155	Example:
156	
157	```fortran
158	      STOP
159	```
160	
161	### END

163	Example:
164	
165	```fortran
166	      END
167	```
168

171	Supported expression forms:
172	
173	- integer literal
174	- scalar variable
175	- array element reference
176	- unary minus
177	- binary `+ - * /`
178	- parentheses
179	
180	Examples:

185	      SUM = SUM + I
186	      X = -I
187	```

193	All variables must be explicitly declared.
194	
195	FTI-0 does not initially support implicit typing.
196	
197	### Types
198	
199	Only integer values are supported.
200	
201	### Arrays
202	
203	Only one-dimensional arrays are supported in FTI-0.
204	
205	### Division
206	
207	Integer division semantics are target-defined but intended to behave as truncation toward zero.
208	
209	## Labels
210	
211	Labels are numeric and appear in columns 1-5.
212	
213	Example:
214	
215	```fortran

217	```

file.content:
# FTI-0 Language Subset

## Overview

FTI-0 is an educational, integer-only, fixed-form subset inspired by classic FORTRAN.

It is intentionally smaller and simpler than historical FORTRAN dialects. The goal is to support a teachable compiler pipeline rather than to reproduce every legacy feature or edge case.

## Source format

FTI-0 source is fixed-form.

### Columns

- columns 1-5: optional numeric statement label
- column 6: continuation marker
- columns 7-72: statement body
- comment lines begin with `C` or `*` in column 1

Anything beyond column 72 is ignored in version 0.

## Case

The initial implementation may normalize identifiers and keywords to uppercase.

## Program structure

A small program typically has the form:

```fortran
      PROGRAM HELLO
      INTEGER I
      I = 42
      PRINT *, I
      STOP
      END
```

## Declarations

### INTEGER

Declares integer scalar variables.

Example:

```fortran
      INTEGER I, N, SUM
```

### DIMENSION

Declares one-dimensional integer arrays.

Example:

```fortran
      DIMENSION A(10)
```

In FTI-0, arrays are:

- integer arrays
- one-dimensional only
- indexed from 1 to extent

## Statements

### PROGRAM

Defines program name.

Example:

```fortran
      PROGRAM SUM10
```

### Assignment

Example:

```fortran
      I = 3
      SUM = SUM + I
      A(I) = I * 10
```

### GOTO

Example:

```fortran
      GOTO 100
```

### IF (...) GOTO

FTI-0 initially supports the simple form:

```fortran
      IF (expr) GOTO 100
```

Semantics:
- if `expr` is nonzero, branch to label 100

### DO

Counted DO with optional default step of 1.

Example:

```fortran
      DO 100 I = 1, 10
```

A later revision may add explicit step:

```fortran
      DO 100 I = 1, 10, 2
```

### CONTINUE

Example:

```fortran
  100 CONTINUE
```

### PRINT

Only list-directed style is initially supported.

Example:

```fortran
      PRINT *, I
      PRINT *, I, SUM, A(3)
```

### READ

Only list-directed style is initially supported.

Example:

```fortran
      READ *, I, N
```

### STOP

Example:

```fortran
      STOP
```

### END

Example:

```fortran
      END
```

## Expressions

Supported expression forms:

- integer literal
- scalar variable
- array element reference
- unary minus
- binary `+ - * /`
- parentheses

Examples:

```fortran
      I = 1
      N = (A(3) + 2) * 5
      SUM = SUM + I
      X = -I
```

## Semantics

### Variables

All variables must be explicitly declared.

FTI-0 does not initially support implicit typing.

### Types

Only integer values are supported.

### Arrays

Only one-dimensional arrays are supported in FTI-0.

### Division

Integer division semantics are target-defined but intended to behave as truncation toward zero.

## Labels

Labels are numeric and appear in columns 1-5.

Example:

```fortran
  100 CONTINUE
```

## Unsupported features

FTI-0 does not initially support:

- floating point
- `REAL`, `DOUBLE PRECISION`
- `COMMON`
- `EQUIVALENCE`
- `FORMAT`
- `DATA`
- arithmetic IF
- computed GOTO
- statement functions
- subroutines and functions
- multidimensional arrays
- character or string types

## Example programs

### Hello

```fortran
      PROGRAM HELLO
      INTEGER I
      I = 42
      PRINT *, I
      STOP
      END
```

### Sum 1 to 10

```fortran
      PROGRAM SUM10
      INTEGER I, S
      S = 0
      DO 100 I = 1, 10
      S = S + I
  100 CONTINUE
      PRINT *, S
      STOP
      END
```

content.content:
1	# PL/SW Mapping
2	
3	## Overview
4	
5	FTI-0 compiles to PL/SW as its initial target language.
6	
7	This document defines the source-to-target mapping contract. The mapping is intended to produce readable PL/SW suitable for inspection and debugging.
8	
9	## Design goals
10	
11	The mapping should:
12	
13	- preserve source intent clearly
14	- avoid unnecessary temporary values where possible
15	- generate stable names
16	- isolate runtime-dependent behavior in helper calls
17	
18	## Naming conventions
19	
20	### User variables
21	
22	User variables are mapped with an `F_` prefix.
23	
24	Examples:
25	
26	- `I` -> `F_I`
27	- `N` -> `F_N`
28	- `SUM` -> `F_SUM`
29	- `A` -> `F_A`
30	
31	### Source labels
32	
33	FORTRAN source labels map to PL/SW labels with an `L_` prefix.
34	
35	Example:
36	
37	- `100` -> `L_100`
38	
39	### Generated internal labels
40	
41	Generated compiler labels use a distinct naming scheme such as:
42	
43	- `L_DO_TEST_1`
44	- `L_DO_EXIT_1`
45	
46	### Temporaries
47	
48	Compiler-generated temporaries use:
49	
50	- `T_1`
51	- `T_2`
52	
53	## Declarations
54	
55	### INTEGER scalar
56	
57	FTI-0 source:
58	
59	```fortran
60	      INTEGER I
61	```
62	
63	Conceptual PL/SW target:
64	
65	```plsw
66	DCL F_I FIXED BIN(24);
67	```
68	
69	Exact PL/SW declaration syntax may be adjusted to match supported PL/SW rules.
70	
71	### DIMENSION array
72	
73	FTI-0 source:
74	
75	```fortran
76	      DIMENSION A(10)
77	```
78	
79	Conceptual PL/SW target:
80	
81	```plsw
82	DCL F_A(10) FIXED BIN(24);
83	```
84	
85	## Assignment mapping
86	
87	FTI-0 source:
88	
89	```fortran
90	      I = N + 1
91	```
92	
93	PL/SW:
94	
95	```plsw
96	F_I = F_N + 1;
97	```
98	
99	Array assignment:
100	
101	```fortran
102	      A(I) = I * 10
103	```
104	
105	PL/SW:
106	
107	```plsw
108	F_A(F_I) = F_I * 10;
109	```
110	
111	## Branch mapping
112	
113	### GOTO
114	
115	FTI-0 source:
116	
117	```fortran
118	      GOTO 100
119	```
120	
121	PL/SW:
122	
123	```plsw
124	GOTO L_100;
125	```
126

file.content:
# PL/SW Mapping

## Overview

FTI-0 compiles to PL/SW as its initial target language.

This document defines the source-to-target mapping contract. The mapping is intended to produce readable PL/SW suitable for inspection and debugging.

## Design goals

The mapping should:

- preserve source intent clearly
- avoid unnecessary temporary values where possible
- generate stable names
- isolate runtime-dependent behavior in helper calls

## Naming conventions

### User variables

User variables are mapped with an `F_` prefix.

Examples:

- `I` -> `F_I`
- `N` -> `F_N`
- `SUM` -> `F_SUM`
- `A` -> `F_A`

### Source labels

FORTRAN source labels map to PL/SW labels with an `L_` prefix.

Example:

- `100` -> `L_100`

### Generated internal labels

Generated compiler labels use a distinct naming scheme such as:

- `L_DO_TEST_1`
- `L_DO_EXIT_1`

### Temporaries

Compiler-generated temporaries use:

- `T_1`
- `T_2`

## Declarations

### INTEGER scalar

FTI-0 source:

```fortran
      INTEGER I
```

Conceptual PL/SW target:

```plsw
DCL F_I FIXED BIN(24);
```

Exact PL/SW declaration syntax may be adjusted to match supported PL/SW rules.

### DIMENSION array

FTI-0 source:

```fortran
      DIMENSION A(10)
```

Conceptual PL/SW target:

```plsw
DCL F_A(10) FIXED BIN(24);
```

## Assignment mapping

FTI-0 source:

```fortran
      I = N + 1
```

PL/SW:

```plsw
F_I = F_N + 1;
```

Array assignment:

```fortran
      A(I) = I * 10
```

PL/SW:

```plsw
F_A(F_I) = F_I * 10;
```

## Branch mapping

### GOTO

FTI-0 source:

```fortran
      GOTO 100
```

PL/SW:

```plsw
GOTO L_100;
```

### IF (...) GOTO

FTI-0 source:

```fortran
      IF (I) GOTO 100
```

PL/SW:

```plsw
IF F_I <> 0 THEN GOTO L_100;
```

Exact relational syntax may be adapted to actual PL/SW supported syntax.

## DO loop mapping

FTI-0 source:

```fortran
      DO 100 I = 1, N
```

Conceptual PL/SW:

```plsw
F_I = 1;
L_DO_TEST_1:
IF F_I > F_N THEN GOTO L_DO_EXIT_1;
...
L_100:
F_I = F_I + 1;
GOTO L_DO_TEST_1;
L_DO_EXIT_1:
```

The compiler may emit equivalent structure with different generated labels.

## I/O mapping

FTI-0 uses runtime helper routines for I/O.

### PRINT

FTI-0 source:

```fortran
      PRINT *, I, SUM
```

Conceptual PL/SW:

```plsw
CALL FTI_PRINT_INT(F_I);
CALL FTI_PRINT_INT(F_SUM);
CALL FTI_PRINT_NL();
```

### READ

FTI-0 source:

```fortran
      READ *, I, N
```

Conceptual PL/SW:

```plsw
CALL FTI_READ_INT(F_I);
CALL FTI_READ_INT(F_N);
```

### STOP

FTI-0 source:

```fortran
      STOP
```

Conceptual PL/SW:

```plsw
CALL FTI_STOP();
```

## Runtime contract

The initial runtime surface is expected to contain helpers such as:

- `FTI_PRINT_INT`
- `FTI_PRINT_NL`
- `FTI_READ_INT`
- `FTI_STOP`

These may later be expanded or renamed based on PL/SW conventions.

## Integer semantics

FTI-0 is integer-only.

The exact storage width may depend on PL/SW and target runtime constraints, but the likely practical execution model is a signed integer aligned with COR24-oriented runtime assumptions.

## Source comments

The emitter may optionally preserve original source lines as comments in generated output to aid debugging.

Example:

```plsw
/* FTI-0: I = N + 1 */
F_I = F_N + 1;
```

## Unsupported mappings for now

No initial mapping is defined for:

- floating point
- formatted I/O
- subprogram linkage
- `COMMON`
- `EQUIVALENCE`
- multidimensional arrays

content.text:
Heads up: `examples/hello.f` already exists, but it's a hello-world in name only — it prints the integer `42`, not a string. A true `PRINT *, 'Hello, World!'` is outside the current FTI-0 spec, which is explicitly integer-only and lists "character or string types" as unsupported (`docs/subset-fti0.md:234`).

Here's what would need to change to support a real string hello-world:

**FTI-0 language (`docs/subset-fti0.md`)**
- A string-literal lexical form. The historically right choice for educational fixed-form is apostrophe-delimited `'Hello, World!'` with `''` as the embedded-quote escape. (Hollerith `13HHello, World!` is the alternative — uglier, more historical, but no scanner state for the closing delimiter.)
- An extension to the `PRINT` list so an item may be a string literal, not just an integer expression. Minimal scope: literals in `PRINT` only — no `CHARACTER` variables, no concatenation, no `LEN`.
- Remove "character or string types" from the unsupported list, or qualify it ("only as immediate `PRINT` arguments").

**SNOBOL4 compiler (`snobol4/src/`)**
- `normalize.sno` — fixed-form scanner needs to recognize apostrophe-delimited literals inside cols 7–72, handle `''` escape, and decide policy for an unterminated literal at column 72 (reject, or allow continuation).
- `classify.sno` / a print-list parser — accept a literal as a print-list item alongside int expressions.
- A new token kind (`STR`) plus diagnostics in `errors.sno` (unterminated literal, stray apostrophe).
- `emit_plsw.sno` — emit `CALL FTI_PRINT_STR('...')` with PL/SW string-escaping rules applied.
- SNOBOL4 itself needs no new language features — it's a string-processing language, so this is all just new compiler logic.

**PL/SW runtime (`plsw/runtime/fti0_runtime.msw`)**
- Add `FTI_PRINT_STR(s)` — writes a string with no trailing newline, parallel to `FTI_PRINT_INT`. (The runtime file is currently a stub with only `END`, so `FTI_PRINT_INT`/`FTI_PRINT_NL`/etc. also don't exist yet — `FTI_PRINT_STR` would land alongside them whenever the runtime gets fleshed out.)

Given that, three reasonable paths — which do you want?

# FTI-0 Language Subset

## Overview

FTI-0 is an educational, integer-only, fixed-form subset inspired by classic FORTRAN.

It is intentionally smaller and simpler than historical FORTRAN dialects. The goal is to support a teachable compiler pipeline rather than to reproduce every legacy feature or edge case.

## Source format

FTI-0 source is fixed-form.

### Columns

- columns 1-5: optional numeric statement label
- column 6: continuation marker
- columns 7-72: statement body
- comment lines begin with `C` or `*` in column 1

Anything beyond column 72 is ignored in version 0.

## Case

The initial implementation may normalize identifiers and keywords to uppercase.

## Program structure

A small program typically has the form:

```fortran
      PROGRAM HELLO
      INTEGER I
      I = 42
      PRINT *, I
      STOP
      END
```

## Declarations

### INTEGER

Declares integer scalar variables.

Example:

```fortran
      INTEGER I, N, SUM
```

### DIMENSION

Declares one-dimensional integer arrays.

Example:

```fortran
      DIMENSION A(10)
```

In FTI-0, arrays are:

- integer arrays
- one-dimensional only
- indexed from 1 to extent

## Statements

### PROGRAM

Defines program name.

Example:

```fortran
      PROGRAM SUM10
```

### Assignment

Example:

```fortran
      I = 3
      SUM = SUM + I
      A(I) = I * 10
```

### GOTO

Example:

```fortran
      GOTO 100
```

### IF (...) GOTO

FTI-0 initially supports the simple form:

```fortran
      IF (expr) GOTO 100
```

Semantics:
- if `expr` is nonzero, branch to label 100

### DO

Counted DO with optional default step of 1.

Example:

```fortran
      DO 100 I = 1, 10
```

A later revision may add explicit step:

```fortran
      DO 100 I = 1, 10, 2
```

### CONTINUE

Example:

```fortran
  100 CONTINUE
```

### PRINT

Only list-directed style is initially supported.

Example:

```fortran
      PRINT *, I
      PRINT *, I, SUM, A(3)
```

### READ

Only list-directed style is initially supported.

Example:

```fortran
      READ *, I, N
```

### STOP

Example:

```fortran
      STOP
```

### END

Example:

```fortran
      END
```

## Expressions

Supported expression forms:

- integer literal
- scalar variable
- array element reference
- unary minus
- binary `+ - * /`
- parentheses

Examples:

```fortran
      I = 1
      N = (A(3) + 2) * 5
      SUM = SUM + I
      X = -I
```

## Semantics

### Variables

All variables must be explicitly declared.

FTI-0 does not initially support implicit typing.

### Types

Only integer values are supported.

### Arrays

Only one-dimensional arrays are supported in FTI-0.

### Division

Integer division semantics are target-defined but intended to behave as truncation toward zero.

## Labels

Labels are numeric and appear in columns 1-5.

Example:

```fortran
  100 CONTINUE
```

## Unsupported features

FTI-0 does not initially support:

- floating point
- `REAL`, `DOUBLE PRECISION`
- `COMMON`
- `EQUIVALENCE`
- `FORMAT`
- `DATA`
- arithmetic IF
- computed GOTO
- statement functions
- subroutines and functions
- multidimensional arrays
- character or string types

## Example programs

### Hello

```fortran
      PROGRAM HELLO
      INTEGER I
      I = 42
      PRINT *, I
      STOP
      END
```

### Sum 1 to 10

```fortran
      PROGRAM SUM10
      INTEGER I, S
      S = 0
      DO 100 I = 1, 10
      S = S + I
  100 CONTINUE
      PRINT *, S
      STOP
      END
```

```

## Semantics
toolUseResult.newString:
X = -I
```

## String literals

A string literal is a sequence of characters enclosed in apostrophes:

```fortran
      'Hello, World!'
```

An embedded apostrophe is written as two consecutive apostrophes:

```fortran
      'IT''S A LITERAL'
```

In FTI-0, string literals are permitted **only as immediate `PRINT` arguments**. There are no `CHARACTER` variables, no string-valued expressions, no concatenation, and no `LEN`.

A literal must begin and end on the same source line, and cannot extend past column 72.

Unlike identifiers and keywords, the contents of a string literal are not normalized — case is preserved exactly as written.

## Semantics

- floating point
- `REAL`, `DOUBLE PRECISION`
- `COMMON`
- `EQUIVALENCE`
- `FORMAT`
- `DATA`
- arithmetic IF
- computed GOTO
- statement functions
- subroutines and functions
- multidimensional arrays
- character or string types

```fortran

```

  100 CONTINUE

# FTI-0 Language Subset

## Overview

FTI-0 is an educational, integer-only, fixed-form subset inspired by classic FORTRAN.

It is intentionally smaller and simpler than historical FORTRAN dialects. The goal is to support a teachable compiler pipeline rather than to reproduce every legacy feature or edge case.

## Source format

FTI-0 source is fixed-form.

### Columns

- columns 1-5: optional numeric statement label
- column 6: continuation marker
- columns 7-72: statement body
- comment lines begin with `C` or `*` in column 1

Anything beyond column 72 is ignored in version 0.

## Case

The initial implementation may normalize identifiers and keywords to uppercase.

## Program structure

A small program typically has the form:

```fortran
      PROGRAM HELLO
      INTEGER I
      I = 42
      PRINT *, I
      STOP
      END
```

## Declarations

### INTEGER

Declares integer scalar variables.

Example:

```fortran
      INTEGER I, N, SUM
```

### DIMENSION

Declares one-dimensional integer arrays.

Example:

```fortran
      DIMENSION A(10)
```

In FTI-0, arrays are:

- integer arrays
- one-dimensional only
- indexed from 1 to extent

## Statements

### PROGRAM

Defines program name.

Example:

```fortran
      PROGRAM SUM10
```

### Assignment

Example:

```fortran
      I = 3
      SUM = SUM + I
      A(I) = I * 10
```

### GOTO

Example:

```fortran
      GOTO 100
```

### IF (...) GOTO

FTI-0 initially supports the simple form:

```fortran
      IF (expr) GOTO 100
```

Semantics:
- if `expr` is nonzero, branch to label 100

### DO

Counted DO with optional default step of 1.

Example:

```fortran
      DO 100 I = 1, 10
```

A later revision may add explicit step:

```fortran
      DO 100 I = 1, 10, 2
```

### CONTINUE

Example:

```fortran
  100 CONTINUE
```

### PRINT

Only list-directed style is initially supported.

A print list item is either an integer expression or a string literal (see [String literals](#string-literals)). Items of either kind may be mixed in one statement.

Example:

```fortran
      PRINT *, I
      PRINT *, I, SUM, A(3)
      PRINT *, 'Hello, World!'
      PRINT *, 'COUNT =', I
```

### READ

Only list-directed style is initially supported.

Example:

```fortran
      READ *, I, N
```

### STOP

Example:

```fortran
      STOP
```

### END

Example:

```fortran
      END
```

## Expressions

Supported expression forms:

- integer literal
- scalar variable
- array element reference
- unary minus
- binary `+ - * /`
- parentheses

Examples:

```fortran
      I = 1
      N = (A(3) + 2) * 5
      SUM = SUM + I
      X = -I
```

## String literals

A string literal is a sequence of characters enclosed in apostrophes:

```fortran
      'Hello, World!'
```

An embedded apostrophe is written as two consecutive apostrophes:

```fortran
      'IT''S A LITERAL'
```

In FTI-0, string literals are permitted **only as immediate `PRINT` arguments**. There are no `CHARACTER` variables, no string-valued expressions, no concatenation, and no `LEN`.

A literal must begin and end on the same source line, and cannot extend past column 72.

Unlike identifiers and keywords, the contents of a string literal are not normalized — case is preserved exactly as written.

## Semantics

### Variables

All variables must be explicitly declared.

FTI-0 does not initially support implicit typing.

### Types

Only integer values are supported.

### Arrays

Only one-dimensional arrays are supported in FTI-0.

### Division

Integer division semantics are target-defined but intended to behave as truncation toward zero.

## Labels

Labels are numeric and appear in columns 1-5.

Example:

```fortran
  100 CONTINUE
```

## Unsupported features

FTI-0 does not initially support:

- floating point
- `REAL`, `DOUBLE PRECISION`
- `COMMON`
- `EQUIVALENCE`
- `FORMAT`
- `DATA`
- arithmetic IF
- computed GOTO
- statement functions
- subroutines and functions
- multidimensional arrays
- character or string types

## Example programs

### Hello

```fortran
      PROGRAM HELLO
      INTEGER I
      I = 42
      PRINT *, I
      STOP
      END
```

### Sum 1 to 10

```fortran
      PROGRAM SUM10
      INTEGER I, S
      S = 0
      DO 100 I = 1, 10
      S = S + I
  100 CONTINUE
      PRINT *, S
      STOP
      END
```

## Overview

## Source format

FTI-0 source is fixed-form.

### Columns

- columns 1-5: optional numeric statement label

Anything beyond column 72 is ignored in version 0.

## Case

The initial implementation may normalize identifiers and keywords to uppercase.

## Program structure

A small program typically has the form:

```fortran
      PROGRAM HELLO
      INTEGER I
      I = 42
      PRINT *, I
      STOP
      END
```

## Declarations

### INTEGER

Declares integer scalar variables.

Example:

```fortran
      INTEGER I, N, SUM
```

### DIMENSION

Declares one-dimensional integer arrays.

Example:

```fortran
      DIMENSION A(10)
```

In FTI-0, arrays are:

- integer arrays
- one-dimensional only
- indexed from 1 to extent

## Statements

### PROGRAM

Defines program name.

Example:

```fortran
      PROGRAM SUM10
```

### Assignment

Example:

```fortran
      I = 3
      SUM = SUM + I
      A(I) = I * 10
```

### GOTO

Example:

```fortran
      GOTO 100
```

### IF (...) GOTO

FTI-0 initially supports the simple form:

```fortran
      IF (expr) GOTO 100
```

Semantics:
- if `expr` is nonzero, branch to label 100

### DO

Counted DO with optional default step of 1.

Example:

```fortran
      DO 100 I = 1, 10
```

A later revision may add explicit step:

```fortran
      DO 100 I = 1, 10, 2
```

### CONTINUE

Example:

```fortran
  100 CONTINUE
```

### PRINT

Only list-directed style is initially supported.

A print list item is either an integer expression or a string literal (see [String literals](#string-literals)). Items of either kind may be mixed in one statement.

Example:

```fortran
      PRINT *, I
      PRINT *, I, SUM, A(3)
      PRINT *, 'Hello, World!'
      PRINT *, 'COUNT =', I
```

### READ

Only list-directed style is initially supported.

Example:

```fortran
      READ *, I, N
```

### STOP

Example:

```fortran
      STOP
```

### END

Example:

```fortran
      END
```

## Expressions

Supported expression forms:

- integer literal
- scalar variable
- array element reference
- unary minus
- binary `+ - * /`
- parentheses

Examples:

```fortran
      I = 1
      N = (A(3) + 2) * 5
      SUM = SUM + I
      X = -I
```

## String literals

A string literal is a sequence of characters enclosed in apostrophes:

```fortran
      'Hello, World!'

```fortran
      'IT''S A LITERAL'
```

A literal must begin and end on the same source line, and cannot extend past column 72.

Unlike identifiers and keywords, the contents of a string literal are not normalized — case is preserved exactly as written.

## Semantics

### Variables

All variables must be explicitly declared.

FTI-0 does not initially support implicit typing.

### Types

Only integer values are supported.

### Arrays

Only one-dimensional arrays are supported in FTI-0.

### Division

Integer division semantics are target-defined but intended to behave as truncation toward zero.

## Labels

Labels are numeric and appear in columns 1-5.

```fortran

FTI-0 does not initially support:

- floating point
- `REAL`, `DOUBLE PRECISION`

- `EQUIVALENCE`

- `DATA`

- statement functions
- subroutines and functions
- multidimensional arrays

```fortran
      PROGRAM HELLO

# PL/SW Mapping

## Overview

FTI-0 compiles to PL/SW as its initial target language.

This document defines the source-to-target mapping contract. The mapping is intended to produce readable PL/SW suitable for inspection and debugging.

## Design goals

The mapping should:

- preserve source intent clearly
- avoid unnecessary temporary values where possible
- generate stable names
- isolate runtime-dependent behavior in helper calls

## Naming conventions

### User variables

User variables are mapped with an `F_` prefix.

Examples:

- `I` -> `F_I`
- `N` -> `F_N`
- `SUM` -> `F_SUM`
- `A` -> `F_A`

### Source labels

FORTRAN source labels map to PL/SW labels with an `L_` prefix.

Example:

- `100` -> `L_100`

### Generated internal labels

Generated compiler labels use a distinct naming scheme such as:

- `L_DO_TEST_1`
- `L_DO_EXIT_1`

### Temporaries

Compiler-generated temporaries use:

- `T_1`
- `T_2`

## Declarations

### INTEGER scalar

FTI-0 source:

```fortran
      INTEGER I
```

Conceptual PL/SW target:

```plsw
DCL F_I FIXED BIN(24);
```

Exact PL/SW declaration syntax may be adjusted to match supported PL/SW rules.

### DIMENSION array

FTI-0 source:

```fortran
      DIMENSION A(10)
```

Conceptual PL/SW target:

```plsw
DCL F_A(10) FIXED BIN(24);
```

## Assignment mapping

FTI-0 source:

```fortran
      I = N + 1
```

PL/SW:

```plsw
F_I = F_N + 1;
```

Array assignment:

```fortran
      A(I) = I * 10
```

PL/SW:

```plsw
F_A(F_I) = F_I * 10;
```

## Branch mapping

### GOTO

FTI-0 source:

```fortran
      GOTO 100
```

PL/SW:

```plsw
GOTO L_100;
```

### IF (...) GOTO

FTI-0 source:

```fortran
      IF (I) GOTO 100
```

PL/SW:

```plsw
IF F_I <> 0 THEN GOTO L_100;
```

Exact relational syntax may be adapted to actual PL/SW supported syntax.

## DO loop mapping

FTI-0 source:

```fortran
      DO 100 I = 1, N
```

Conceptual PL/SW:

```plsw
F_I = 1;
L_DO_TEST_1:
IF F_I > F_N THEN GOTO L_DO_EXIT_1;
...
L_100:
F_I = F_I + 1;
GOTO L_DO_TEST_1;
L_DO_EXIT_1:
```

The compiler may emit equivalent structure with different generated labels.

## I/O mapping

FTI-0 uses runtime helper routines for I/O.

### PRINT

FTI-0 source:

```fortran
      PRINT *, I, SUM
```

Conceptual PL/SW:

```plsw
CALL FTI_PRINT_INT(F_I);
CALL FTI_PRINT_INT(F_SUM);
CALL FTI_PRINT_NL();
```

### READ

FTI-0 source:

```fortran
      READ *, I, N
```

Conceptual PL/SW:

```plsw
CALL FTI_READ_INT(F_I);
CALL FTI_READ_INT(F_N);
```

### STOP

FTI-0 source:

```fortran
      STOP
```

Conceptual PL/SW:

```plsw
CALL FTI_STOP();
```

## Runtime contract

The initial runtime surface is expected to contain helpers such as:

- `FTI_PRINT_INT`
- `FTI_PRINT_NL`
- `FTI_READ_INT`
- `FTI_STOP`

These may later be expanded or renamed based on PL/SW conventions.

## Integer semantics

FTI-0 is integer-only.

The exact storage width may depend on PL/SW and target runtime constraints, but the likely practical execution model is a signed integer aligned with COR24-oriented runtime assumptions.

## Source comments

The emitter may optionally preserve original source lines as comments in generated output to aid debugging.

Example:

```plsw
/* FTI-0: I = N + 1 */
F_I = F_N + 1;
```

## Unsupported mappings for now

No initial mapping is defined for:

- floating point
- formatted I/O
- subprogram linkage
- `COMMON`
- `EQUIVALENCE`
- multidimensional arrays

```

PL/SW:

```plsw
IF F_I <> 0 THEN GOTO L_100;
```

Exact relational syntax may be adapted to actual PL/SW supported syntax.

## DO loop mapping

FTI-0 source:

```fortran
      DO 100 I = 1, N
```

Conceptual PL/SW:

```plsw
F_I = 1;
L_DO_TEST_1:
IF F_I > F_N THEN GOTO L_DO_EXIT_1;
...
L_100:
F_I = F_I + 1;
GOTO L_DO_TEST_1;
L_DO_EXIT_1:
```

The compiler may emit equivalent structure with different generated labels.

## I/O mapping

FTI-0 uses runtime helper routines for I/O.

### PRINT

FTI-0 source:

```fortran
      PRINT *, I, SUM
```

Conceptual PL/SW:

```plsw
CALL FTI_PRINT_INT(F_I);
CALL FTI_PRINT_INT(F_SUM);
CALL FTI_PRINT_NL();
```

A print list item that is a string literal lowers to `FTI_PRINT_STR`:

FTI-0 source:

```fortran
      PRINT *, 'Hello, World!'
```

Conceptual PL/SW:

```plsw
CALL FTI_PRINT_STR('Hello, World!');
CALL FTI_PRINT_NL();
```

Mixed lists lower in source order:

FTI-0 source:

```fortran
      PRINT *, 'COUNT =', I
```

Conceptual PL/SW:

```plsw
CALL FTI_PRINT_STR('COUNT =');
CALL FTI_PRINT_INT(F_I);
CALL FTI_PRINT_NL();
```

### READ

FTI-0 source:

```fortran
      READ *, I, N
```

Conceptual PL/SW:

```plsw
CALL FTI_READ_INT(F_I);
CALL FTI_READ_INT(F_N);
```

### STOP

FTI-0 source:

```fortran
      STOP
```

Conceptual PL/SW:

```plsw
CALL FTI_STOP();
```

## Runtime contract

The initial runtime surface is expected to contain helpers such as:

- `FTI_PRINT_INT`
- `FTI_PRINT_NL`
- `FTI_READ_INT`
- `FTI_STOP`

These may later be expanded or renamed based on PL/SW conventions.

## Integer semantics

FTI-0 is integer-only.

The exact storage width may depend on PL/SW and target runtime constraints, but the likely practical execution model is a signed integer aligned with COR24-oriented runtime assumptions.

## Source comments

The emitter may optionally preserve original source lines as comments in generated output to aid debugging.

Example:

```plsw
/* FTI-0: I = N + 1 */
F_I = F_N + 1;
```

## Unsupported mappings for now

No initial mapping is defined for:

- floating point
- formatted I/O
- subprogram linkage
- `COMMON`
- `EQUIVALENCE`
- multidimensional arrays

The README confirms this — current status is "the project structure / subset definition / mapping contract / milestone plan." No code generation is part of the first milestone (`README.md:68`). The spec edits I just made are *also* documentation only — I called that out explicitly when you picked the approach.

So `examples/hello.f` is a source file the future compiler is *meant* to accept; running it end-to-end isn't possible at this milestone.

If you want forward motion, three real options:

1. **Implement milestone 1** — make `driver.sno` actually normalize and classify `examples/hello.f`, dump the records. This needs SNOBOL4 installed first (`docs/tools.md` may say which implementation; I haven't read it). Output would be parsed-statement records, not "Hello, World!" — but it'd be a real run.
2. **Implement just enough to run hello.f** — driver → emit PL/SW → also need a PL/SW interpreter to execute, plus the runtime helpers stubbed in. Much larger; spans all five compiler phase files plus the runtime.
3. **Stop here.** The spec change stands; running waits until the compiler exists.

content.content:
1	# Architecture
2	
3	## Overview
4	
5	FTI-0 is a source-to-source compiler:
6	
7	```text
8	FTI-0 source
9	  -> normalization
10	  -> statement classification
11	  -> expression parsing
12	  -> semantic analysis
13	  -> lowering
14	  -> PL/SW emission
15	```
16	
17	The implementation language is SNOBOL4.  
18	The emitted target language is PL/SW.
19	
20	## Architectural priorities
21	
22	The architecture prioritizes:
23	
24	- phase separation
25	- inspectability
26	- deterministic output
27	- explicit intermediate representations
28	- easy extension in later revisions
29	
30	## Compiler phases
31	
32	### 1. Driver
33	
34	Responsibilities:
35	
36	- read options
37	- open input/output files
38	- coordinate passes
39	- select dump modes
40	- report final success/failure
41	
42	Primary file:
43	- `snobol4/src/driver.sno`
44	
45	### 2. Normalization
46	
47	Responsibilities:
48	
49	- read fixed-form physical lines
50	- detect comments
51	- extract label field
52	- process continuation
53	- assemble logical statements
54	- preserve source line mapping
55	
56	Output:
57	- normalized statement records
58	
59	Primary file:
60	- `snobol4/src/normalize.sno`
61	
62	### 3. Statement classification
63	
64	Responsibilities:
65	
66	- identify statement kind
67	- split major fields
68	- route expressions and lists to specialized parsing
69	
70	Recognized statement families include:
71	
72	- program start
73	- declarations
74	- assignment
75	- branch
76	- loop
77	- I/O
78	- terminators
79	
80	Primary file:
81	- `snobol4/src/classify.sno`
82	
83	### 4. Expression parsing
84	
85	Responsibilities:
86	
87	- tokenize expressions
88	- parse precedence and parentheses
89	- construct expression trees or equivalent records
90	
91	Supported expression forms in FTI-0:
92	
93	- integer literals
94	- scalar references
95	- array references
96	- unary minus
97	- `+ - * /`
98	
99	Primary file:
100	- `snobol4/src/expr.sno`
101	
102	### 5. Semantic analysis
103	
104	Responsibilities:
105	
106	- symbol declaration tracking
107	- scalar vs array validation
108	- label definition/reference tracking
109	- duplicate declaration detection
110	- undefined label detection
111	- statement-specific checks
112	
113	Primary files:
114	- `snobol4/src/symbols.sno`
115	- `snobol4/src/labels.sno`
116	
117	### 6. Lowering
118	
119	Responsibilities:
120	
121	- convert parsed statements into a smaller normalized control-flow-oriented form
122	- make loop expansion explicit
123	- assign generated temporary names and internal labels
124	
125	Lowering is deliberately simple and not intended as a stable external IR.
126	
127	Primary file:
128	- `snobol4/src/lower.sno`
129	
130	### 7. PL/SW emission
131	
132	Responsibilities:
133	
134	- map symbols to PL/SW declarations
135	- emit readable labels and branches
136	- emit runtime calls for basic I/O and stop
137	- preserve useful comments/source mapping when practical
138	
139	Primary file:
140	- `snobol4/src/emit_plsw.sno`
141	
142	## Data flow
143	
144	### Source input
145	
146	Input is a fixed-form `.f` source file.
147	
148	### Intermediate forms
149	
150	The compiler uses several conceptual representations:
151	
152	1. physical line records
153	2. logical normalized statement records
154	3. classified statement records
155	4. expression nodes
156	5. semantic tables
157	6. lowered normalized statements
158	7. PL/SW output text
159	
160	## Error model
161	
162	Errors should be reported with:
163	
164	- phase name
165	- source line number if available
166	- original or normalized source text if useful
167	- concise explanation
168	
169	Example classes:
170	
171	- invalid fixed-form source
172	- unknown statement form
173	- malformed expression
174	- duplicate declaration
175	- use of undeclared variable
176	- undefined label
177	- type/class mismatch
178	
179	## Observability modes
180	
181	Recommended dump modes:
182	
183	- `-dump-lines`
184	- `-dump-statements`
185	- `-dump-symbols`
186	- `-dump-labels`
187	- `-dump-lowered`
188	- `-dump-plsw`
189	
190	These modes are essential for incremental compiler development.
191	
192	## Target architecture relationship
193	
194	FTI-0 does not directly target COR24 machine code in the first stage.
195	
196	Instead:
197	
198	```text
199	FTI-0
200	  -> PL/SW
201	  -> later PL/SW toolchain stages
202	```
203	
204	This keeps the frontend stable while backend and runtime choices evolve.
205	
206	## Rationale for PL/SW target
207	
208	PL/SW is chosen as the first target because it provides:
209	
210	- inspectable output
211	- alignment with the broader toolchain
212	- a better first stage than direct assembly emission
213	- a future path toward integer and later floating-point runtime support
214

file.content:
# Architecture

## Overview

FTI-0 is a source-to-source compiler:

```text
FTI-0 source
  -> normalization
  -> statement classification
  -> expression parsing
  -> semantic analysis
  -> lowering
  -> PL/SW emission
```

The implementation language is SNOBOL4.  
The emitted target language is PL/SW.

## Architectural priorities

The architecture prioritizes:

- phase separation
- inspectability
- deterministic output
- explicit intermediate representations
- easy extension in later revisions

## Compiler phases

### 1. Driver

Responsibilities:

- read options
- open input/output files
- coordinate passes
- select dump modes
- report final success/failure

Primary file:
- `snobol4/src/driver.sno`

### 2. Normalization

Responsibilities:

- read fixed-form physical lines
- detect comments
- extract label field
- process continuation
- assemble logical statements
- preserve source line mapping

Output:
- normalized statement records

Primary file:
- `snobol4/src/normalize.sno`

### 3. Statement classification

Responsibilities:

- identify statement kind
- split major fields
- route expressions and lists to specialized parsing

Recognized statement families include:

- program start
- declarations
- assignment
- branch
- loop
- I/O
- terminators

Primary file:
- `snobol4/src/classify.sno`

### 4. Expression parsing

Responsibilities:

- tokenize expressions
- parse precedence and parentheses
- construct expression trees or equivalent records

Supported expression forms in FTI-0:

- integer literals
- scalar references
- array references
- unary minus
- `+ - * /`

Primary file:
- `snobol4/src/expr.sno`

### 5. Semantic analysis

Responsibilities:

- symbol declaration tracking
- scalar vs array validation
- label definition/reference tracking
- duplicate declaration detection
- undefined label detection
- statement-specific checks

Primary files:
- `snobol4/src/symbols.sno`
- `snobol4/src/labels.sno`

### 6. Lowering

Responsibilities:

- convert parsed statements into a smaller normalized control-flow-oriented form
- make loop expansion explicit
- assign generated temporary names and internal labels

Lowering is deliberately simple and not intended as a stable external IR.

Primary file:
- `snobol4/src/lower.sno`

### 7. PL/SW emission

Responsibilities:

- map symbols to PL/SW declarations
- emit readable labels and branches
- emit runtime calls for basic I/O and stop
- preserve useful comments/source mapping when practical

Primary file:
- `snobol4/src/emit_plsw.sno`

## Data flow

### Source input

Input is a fixed-form `.f` source file.

### Intermediate forms

The compiler uses several conceptual representations:

1. physical line records
2. logical normalized statement records
3. classified statement records
4. expression nodes
5. semantic tables
6. lowered normalized statements
7. PL/SW output text

## Error model

Errors should be reported with:

- phase name
- source line number if available
- original or normalized source text if useful
- concise explanation

Example classes:

- invalid fixed-form source
- unknown statement form
- malformed expression
- duplicate declaration
- use of undeclared variable
- undefined label
- type/class mismatch

## Observability modes

Recommended dump modes:

- `-dump-lines`
- `-dump-statements`
- `-dump-symbols`
- `-dump-labels`
- `-dump-lowered`
- `-dump-plsw`

These modes are essential for incremental compiler development.

## Target architecture relationship

FTI-0 does not directly target COR24 machine code in the first stage.

Instead:

```text
FTI-0
  -> PL/SW
  -> later PL/SW toolchain stages
```

This keeps the frontend stable while backend and runtime choices evolve.

## Rationale for PL/SW target

PL/SW is chosen as the first target because it provides:

- inspectable output
- alignment with the broader toolchain
- a better first stage than direct assembly emission
- a future path toward integer and later floating-point runtime support

5	This document describes the concrete design of the FTI-0 compiler.
6	
7	The compiler is intentionally small and explicit. It does not rely on hidden or overly clever transformations. The design emphasizes named phases, normalized records, and readable generated PL/SW.
8	
9	## Source form
10	
11	FTI-0 uses a restricted fixed-form source layout.
12	
13	### Columns
14	
15	- columns 1-5: optional numeric label
16	- column 6: continuation marker
17	- columns 7-72: statement text
18	- column 1 containing `C` or `*`: comment line
19	
20	Anything beyond column 72 is ignored in the first implementation.
21	
22	## Statement model
23	
24	Each logical statement should be represented internally as a record with at least:
25	
26	- statement id
27	- source line mapping
28	- optional label
29	- statement kind
30	- statement-specific payload
31	
32	Example conceptual record:
33	
34	```text
35	stmt_id=7
36	label=100
37	kind=DO
38	payload:
39	  var=I
40	  start_expr=1
41	  limit_expr=N
42	  step_expr=1
43	  end_label=100
44	```
45	
46	## Statement kinds
47	
48	Initial kinds:
49	
50	- `PROGRAM`
51	- `INTEGER_DECL`
52	- `DIMENSION_DECL`
53	- `ASSIGN`
54	- `GOTO`
55	- `IF_GOTO`
56	- `DO`
57	- `CONTINUE`
58	- `PRINT`
59	- `READ`
60	- `STOP`
61	- `END`
62	
63	## Symbol table design
64	
65	Each symbol entry should include:
66	
67	- source name
68	- generated PL/SW name
69	- class: scalar or array
70	- type: integer
71	- array length if applicable
72	- declaration source location
73	
74	Example:
75	
76	```text
77	name=I
78	plsw_name=F_I
79	class=scalar
80	type=integer
81	```
82	
83	For arrays:
84	
85	```text
86	name=A
87	plsw_name=F_A
88	class=array
89	type=integer
90	extent=10
91	```
92	
93	## Label table design
94	
95	Each label entry should include:
96	
97	- FORTRAN label number
98	- generated PL/SW label name
99	- defining statement
100	- references
101	
102	Example:
103	
104	```text
105	fortran_label=100
106	plsw_label=L_100
107	defined_at=stmt_9
108	referenced_by=stmt_4,stmt_8
109	```
110	
111	## Expression design
112	
113	Expressions should be parsed separately from statement classification.
114	
115	Supported node kinds:
116	
117	- integer literal
118	- scalar variable reference
119	- array reference
120	- unary operator
121	- binary operator
122	
123	Supported operators:
124	
125	- unary `-`
126	- binary `+`
127	- binary `-`
128	- binary `*`
129	- binary `/`
130	
131	Parentheses are supported.
132	
133	## Expression parsing approach
134	
135	Recommended implementation approach:
136	
137	- tokenize expression text
138	- convert with precedence handling
139	- build expression nodes
140	
141	Either precedence climbing or shunting-yard is acceptable.  
142	The preferred initial design is **shunting-yard** because it is easy to debug with dump output.
143	
144	## Lowered normalized form
145	
146	Before emission, control-flow-heavy statements should be lowered.
147	
148	Lowered forms may include:
149	
150	- label
151	- assignment
152	- conditional branch
153	- unconditional branch
154	- print call
155	- read call
156	- stop call
157	
158	This form is internal to the compiler and does not need a stable file format.
159	
160	## DO loop lowering
161	
162	A counted DO loop:
163	
164	```fortran
165	      DO 100 I = 1, N
166	```
167	
168	should lower to an explicit sequence conceptually equivalent to:
169	
170	```text
171	I := 1
172	L_DO_TEST_1:
173	if I > N goto L_DO_EXIT_1
174	... body ...
175	L_100:
176	I := I + 1
177	goto L_DO_TEST_1
178	L_DO_EXIT_1:
179	```
180	
181	The exact generated labels may differ, but the semantics must be documented and consistent.
182	
183	## Conditional transfer design
184	
185	For the first version, prefer a simplified conditional transfer form rather than classic arithmetic IF.
186	
187	Recommended supported form:
188	
189	```fortran
190	      IF (expr) GOTO label
191	```
192	
193	with semantics:
194	
195	- branch if `expr` is nonzero
196	
197	Arithmetic IF may be added later as a separate milestone.
198	
199	## I/O design
200	
201	Initial I/O is intentionally small.
202	
203	### Supported forms
204	
205	- `PRINT *, expr-list`
206	- `READ *, var-list`
207	
208	### Emission strategy
209	
210	Generated PL/SW should call runtime helpers such as:
211	
212	- `FTI_PRINT_INT(x)`
213	- `FTI_PRINT_NL()`
214	- `FTI_READ_INT(x)`
215	- `FTI_STOP()`
216	
217	This keeps the generated code simple.
218	
219	## Naming conventions
220	
221	To avoid collisions and keep generated code readable:
222	
223	- user scalar or array names map to `F_<name>`
224	- generated internal labels map to `L_<n>` or `L_<fortran_label>`
225	- generated temporaries map to `T_<n>`
226	
227	Examples:
228	
229	- `I` -> `F_I`
230	- `A` -> `F_A`
231	- source label `100` -> `L_100`
232	
233	## Declaration policy
234	
235	FTI-0 requires explicit declaration of variables through:
236	
237	- `INTEGER`
238	- `DIMENSION`
239	
240	No implicit typing is enabled in the initial version.
241	
242	## Unsupported constructs in design v0
243	
244	Not supported yet:
245	
246	- multidimensional arrays
247	- subroutines
248	- functions
249	- `COMMON`
250	- `EQUIVALENCE`
251	- floating-point types
252	- `FORMAT`
253	- `DATA`
254	- computed GOTO

file.content:
# Design

## Overview

This document describes the concrete design of the FTI-0 compiler.

The compiler is intentionally small and explicit. It does not rely on hidden or overly clever transformations. The design emphasizes named phases, normalized records, and readable generated PL/SW.

## Source form

FTI-0 uses a restricted fixed-form source layout.

### Columns

- columns 1-5: optional numeric label
- column 6: continuation marker
- columns 7-72: statement text
- column 1 containing `C` or `*`: comment line

Anything beyond column 72 is ignored in the first implementation.

## Statement model

Each logical statement should be represented internally as a record with at least:

- statement id
- source line mapping
- optional label
- statement kind
- statement-specific payload

Example conceptual record:

```text
stmt_id=7
label=100
kind=DO
payload:
  var=I
  start_expr=1
  limit_expr=N
  step_expr=1
  end_label=100
```

## Statement kinds

Initial kinds:

- `PROGRAM`
- `INTEGER_DECL`
- `DIMENSION_DECL`
- `ASSIGN`
- `GOTO`
- `IF_GOTO`
- `DO`
- `CONTINUE`
- `PRINT`
- `READ`
- `STOP`
- `END`

## Symbol table design

Each symbol entry should include:

- source name
- generated PL/SW name
- class: scalar or array
- type: integer
- array length if applicable
- declaration source location

Example:

```text
name=I
plsw_name=F_I
class=scalar
type=integer
```

For arrays:

```text
name=A
plsw_name=F_A
class=array
type=integer
extent=10
```

## Label table design

Each label entry should include:

- FORTRAN label number
- generated PL/SW label name
- defining statement
- references

Example:

```text
fortran_label=100
plsw_label=L_100
defined_at=stmt_9
referenced_by=stmt_4,stmt_8
```

## Expression design

Expressions should be parsed separately from statement classification.

Supported node kinds:

- integer literal
- scalar variable reference
- array reference
- unary operator
- binary operator

Supported operators:

- unary `-`
- binary `+`
- binary `-`
- binary `*`
- binary `/`

Parentheses are supported.

## Expression parsing approach

Recommended implementation approach:

- tokenize expression text
- convert with precedence handling
- build expression nodes

Either precedence climbing or shunting-yard is acceptable.  
The preferred initial design is **shunting-yard** because it is easy to debug with dump output.

## Lowered normalized form

Before emission, control-flow-heavy statements should be lowered.

Lowered forms may include:

- label
- assignment
- conditional branch
- unconditional branch
- print call
- read call
- stop call

This form is internal to the compiler and does not need a stable file format.

## DO loop lowering

A counted DO loop:

```fortran
      DO 100 I = 1, N
```

should lower to an explicit sequence conceptually equivalent to:

```text
I := 1
L_DO_TEST_1:
if I > N goto L_DO_EXIT_1
... body ...
L_100:
I := I + 1
goto L_DO_TEST_1
L_DO_EXIT_1:
```

The exact generated labels may differ, but the semantics must be documented and consistent.

## Conditional transfer design

For the first version, prefer a simplified conditional transfer form rather than classic arithmetic IF.

Recommended supported form:

```fortran
      IF (expr) GOTO label
```

with semantics:

- branch if `expr` is nonzero

Arithmetic IF may be added later as a separate milestone.

## I/O design

Initial I/O is intentionally small.

### Supported forms

- `PRINT *, expr-list`
- `READ *, var-list`

### Emission strategy

Generated PL/SW should call runtime helpers such as:

- `FTI_PRINT_INT(x)`
- `FTI_PRINT_NL()`
- `FTI_READ_INT(x)`
- `FTI_STOP()`

This keeps the generated code simple.

## Naming conventions

To avoid collisions and keep generated code readable:

- user scalar or array names map to `F_<name>`
- generated internal labels map to `L_<n>` or `L_<fortran_label>`
- generated temporaries map to `T_<n>`

Examples:

- `I` -> `F_I`
- `A` -> `F_A`
- source label `100` -> `L_100`

## Declaration policy

FTI-0 requires explicit declaration of variables through:

- `INTEGER`
- `DIMENSION`

No implicit typing is enabled in the initial version.

## Unsupported constructs in design v0

Not supported yet:

- multidimensional arrays
- subroutines
- functions
- `COMMON`
- `EQUIVALENCE`
- floating-point types
- `FORMAT`
- `DATA`
- computed GOTO

content.content:
1	# Development Tools
2	
3	This document describes the tools used in the sw-cor24-fortran project.
4	
5	## Core Toolchain
6	
7	| Tool | Purpose | Location |
8	|------|---------|----------|
9	| snobol4 | SNOBOL4 interpreter -- runs the FTI-0 compiler source | `sw-cor24-snobol4` |
10	| plsw | PL/SW transpiler -- converts .plsw/.msw to .s assembly | `sw-cor24-plsw` |
11	| cor24-run | COR24 assembler + emulator | `sw-cor24-emulator` |
12	
13	All COR24 repos live under `~/github/sw-embed/` as siblings.
14	
15	## SNOBOL4 Interpreter
16	
17	The FTI-0 compiler is a SNOBOL4 program. The SNOBOL4 interpreter runs it.
18	
19	```bash
20	# Run the FTI-0 compiler on a source file
21	snobol4 snobol4/src/driver.sno < input.f
22	
23	# Run with dump modes (when implemented)
24	snobol4 snobol4/src/driver.sno -dump-lines < input.f
25	snobol4 snobol4/src/driver.sno -dump-statements < input.f
26	```
27	
28	### SNOBOL4 Key Features Used
29	
30	- Pattern matching for fixed-form source normalization
31	- Tables for symbol tables and label tables
32	- String processing for tokenization and emission
33	- Pattern-driven statement classification
34	
35	## PL/SW Transpiler
36	
37	Converts emitted PL/SW files to COR24 assembly.
38	
39	```bash
40	# Transpile a single PL/SW file
41	plsw output.msw
42	
43	# Transpile with linking
44	plsw main.msw runtime.msw
45	```
46	
47	## COR24 Emulator
48	
49	Assembles and emulates COR24 machine code.
50	
51	```bash
52	# Assemble and run
53	cor24-run program.s
54	
55	# Assemble only
56	cor24-run --assemble program.s -o program.bin
57	```
58	
59	## Development Helpers
60	
61	### markdown-checker
62	
63	Validates that markdown files use ASCII-only characters.
64	
65	```bash
66	# Validate all markdown
67	markdown-checker -f "**/*.md"
68	
69	# Auto-fix tree symbols
70	markdown-checker --fix
71	```
72	
73	Run this before committing any markdown changes.
74	
75	### agentrail
76	
77	Manages the development saga and tracks milestone progress.
78	
79	```bash
80	agentrail status          # Current saga state
81	agentrail history         # All completed steps
82	agentrail plan            # View the plan
83	agentrail next            # Current step + context
84	```
85	
86	## Tool Discovery
87	
88	When working with a new COR24 repo:
89	
90	1. Check `~/github/sw-embed/` for sibling repos
91	2. Look for README.md and build instructions in each repo
92	3. Verify tools are on PATH
93	4. Run `--help` on tools for AI-agent-specific guidance
94	
95	## Build Pipeline
96	
97	The full pipeline from FORTRAN source to execution:
98	
99	```
100	.f source
101	  -> [snobol4 interpreter runs driver.sno]
102	  -> .msw/.plsw files (emitted PL/SW)
103	  -> [plsw transpiler]
104	  -> .s assembly
105	  -> [cor24-run assembler+emulator]
106	  -> execution on COR24
107	```
108	
109	Each stage can be tested independently.

file.content:
# Development Tools

This document describes the tools used in the sw-cor24-fortran project.

## Core Toolchain

| Tool | Purpose | Location |
|------|---------|----------|
| snobol4 | SNOBOL4 interpreter -- runs the FTI-0 compiler source | `sw-cor24-snobol4` |
| plsw | PL/SW transpiler -- converts .plsw/.msw to .s assembly | `sw-cor24-plsw` |
| cor24-run | COR24 assembler + emulator | `sw-cor24-emulator` |

All COR24 repos live under `~/github/sw-embed/` as siblings.

## SNOBOL4 Interpreter

The FTI-0 compiler is a SNOBOL4 program. The SNOBOL4 interpreter runs it.

```bash
# Run the FTI-0 compiler on a source file
snobol4 snobol4/src/driver.sno < input.f

# Run with dump modes (when implemented)
snobol4 snobol4/src/driver.sno -dump-lines < input.f
snobol4 snobol4/src/driver.sno -dump-statements < input.f
```

### SNOBOL4 Key Features Used

- Pattern matching for fixed-form source normalization
- Tables for symbol tables and label tables
- String processing for tokenization and emission
- Pattern-driven statement classification

## PL/SW Transpiler

Converts emitted PL/SW files to COR24 assembly.

```bash
# Transpile a single PL/SW file
plsw output.msw

# Transpile with linking
plsw main.msw runtime.msw
```

## COR24 Emulator

Assembles and emulates COR24 machine code.

```bash
# Assemble and run
cor24-run program.s

# Assemble only
cor24-run --assemble program.s -o program.bin
```

## Development Helpers

### markdown-checker

Validates that markdown files use ASCII-only characters.

```bash
# Validate all markdown
markdown-checker -f "**/*.md"

# Auto-fix tree symbols
markdown-checker --fix
```

Run this before committing any markdown changes.

### agentrail

Manages the development saga and tracks milestone progress.

```bash
agentrail status          # Current saga state
agentrail history         # All completed steps
agentrail plan            # View the plan
agentrail next            # Current step + context
```

## Tool Discovery

When working with a new COR24 repo:

1. Check `~/github/sw-embed/` for sibling repos
2. Look for README.md and build instructions in each repo
3. Verify tools are on PATH
4. Run `--help` on tools for AI-agent-specific guidance

## Build Pipeline

The full pipeline from FORTRAN source to execution:

```
.f source
  -> [snobol4 interpreter runs driver.sno]
  -> .msw/.plsw files (emitted PL/SW)
  -> [plsw transpiler]
  -> .s assembly
  -> [cor24-run assembler+emulator]
  -> execution on COR24
```

Each stage can be tested independently.
