This document describes the tools used in the sw-cor24-fortran project.

## Core Toolchain

All toolchain binaries are PATH-resolved from `$TOOLROOT`
(`/disk1/github/softwarewrighter/devgroup/work/bin/` on the devgroup
host). No sibling-clone setup is required to invoke them.

| Tool | Purpose | Source repo |
|------|---------|-------------|
| `tc24r` | C -> COR24 `.s` | sw-cor24-x-tinyc |
| `cor24-asm` | `.s` -> `.lgo` / `.bin` / `.lst` (with optional `--base-addr`) | sw-cor24-x-assembler |
| `cor24-emu` | run `.lgo`; load raw bytes via `--load-binary` | sw-cor24-emulator |
| `cor24-dbg` | debug `.lgo` | sw-cor24-emulator |
| `link24`, `meta-gen` | PL/SW separate-compilation linker | sw-cor24-plsw |
| `pl-sw` | wraps `cor24-emu --lgo plsw.lgo` | sw-cor24-plsw |
| `snobol4` | wraps `cor24-emu --lgo snobol4.lgo` (after the dcsno bootstrap brief ships -- see `docs/snobol4-blockers.md`) | sw-cor24-snobol4 |

The deprecated `cor24-run` (with its `--run` and `--assemble`
sub-flags) still exists in transition but is slated for retirement;
new code uses `cor24-asm` + `cor24-emu` per the
`dc-migrate-toolchain.md` brief mapping.

## SNOBOL4 Interpreter

The FTI-0 compiler is a SNOBOL4 program. The SNOBOL4 interpreter runs it.

```bash
# Run the FTI-0 compiler on a source file
snobol4 snobol4/src/driver.sno < input.f

# Run with dump modes (when implemented)
snobol4 snobol4/src/driver.sno -dump-lines < input.f
snobol4 snobol4/src/driver.sno -dump-statements < input.f
```

### SNOBOL4 Key Features Used

- Pattern matching for fixed-form source normalization
- Tables for symbol tables and label tables
- String processing for tokenization and emission
- Pattern-driven statement classification

## PL/SW Transpiler

Converts emitted PL/SW files to COR24 assembly.

```bash
# Transpile a single PL/SW file
plsw output.msw

# Transpile with linking
plsw main.msw runtime.msw
```

## COR24 Emulator

Assembles and emulates COR24 machine code.

```bash
# Assemble and run
cor24-run program.s

# Assemble only
cor24-run --assemble program.s -o program.bin
```

## Development Helpers

### markdown-checker

Validates that markdown files use ASCII-only characters.

```bash
# Validate all markdown
markdown-checker -f "**/*.md"

# Auto-fix tree symbols
markdown-checker --fix
```

Run this before committing any markdown changes.

### agentrail

Manages the development saga and tracks milestone progress.

```bash
agentrail status          # Current saga state
agentrail history         # All completed steps
agentrail plan            # View the plan
agentrail next            # Current step + context
```

## Tool Discovery

When working with a new COR24 repo:

1. Check `~/github/sw-embed/` for sibling repos
2. Look for README.md and build instructions in each repo
3. Verify tools are on PATH
4. Run `--help` on tools for AI-agent-specific guidance

## Build Pipeline

The full pipeline from FORTRAN source to execution:

```
.f source
  -> [snobol4 interpreter runs driver.sno]
  -> .msw/.plsw files (emitted PL/SW)
  -> [plsw transpiler]
  -> .s assembly
  -> [cor24-run assembler+emulator]
  -> execution on COR24
```

Each stage can be tested independently.

(or by deriving from `pl-sw`'s install dir if `$TOOLROOT` is unset).

          --speed 0 -n 100000000

- Tables for symbol tables and label tables
- String processing for tokenization and emission

## Build Pipeline

The full pipeline from FORTRAN source to execution on COR24:

```
input.f                 (FTI-0 source)
  -> scripts/fortran    (cor24-emu --lgo snobol4.lgo --uart-file driver.sno -u <input.f>)
  -> .plsw / .msw       (emitted PL/SW)
  -> pl-sw              (cor24-emu --lgo plsw.lgo)
  -> .s                 (COR24 assembly)
  -> cor24-asm          (assembler)
  -> .lgo               (COR24 image)
  -> cor24-emu          (run on the emulator)
```

Each stage can be tested independently. At runtime, every layer is
just `cor24-emu --lgo <some>.lgo` with appropriate UART input -- the
"tower" is purely a build-time dependency graph.

## Toolchain

All toolchain binaries are PATH-resolved from `$TOOLROOT` on the
devgroup host. The "tower" is build-time only -- at runtime every
layer is just `cor24-emu --lgo <some>.lgo` with appropriate UART
input.

| Tool | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `tc24r` | C -> COR24 `.s` |
| `cor24-asm` | `.s` -> `.lgo` / `.bin` |
| `cor24-emu` | run a `.lgo` |
| `pl-sw` | wraps `cor24-emu --lgo plsw.lgo` |
| `snobol4` | wraps `cor24-emu --lgo snobol4.lgo` (after dcsno's bootstrap brief ships) |
| `cor24-run` | deprecated; slated for retirement (see `dc-migrate-toolchain.md`) |

### Build Pipeline

```
input.f
  -> scripts/fortran    (cor24-emu --lgo snobol4.lgo --uart-file driver.sno -u <input.f>)
  -> .plsw / .msw       (emitted PL/SW)
  -> pl-sw              (cor24-emu --lgo plsw.lgo)
  -> .s
  -> cor24-asm
  -> .lgo
  -> cor24-emu
```

The FTI-0 compiler itself is a SNOBOL4 program. The `snobol4` wrapper
on PATH is the bottom of our build pipeline; `scripts/fortran` is our
user-facing tool that wires the user's `.f` source through it. See
`docs/tools.md` for full invocation details and
`docs/snobol4-blockers.md` for the current deployment status of the
`snobol4` wrapper.

### Toolchain Locations (this project validates these)

- `sw-cor24-snobol4` -- SNOBOL4 interpreter (built to `snobol4.lgo`)
- `sw-cor24-plsw` -- PL/SW compiler + linker (built to `plsw.lgo`)
- `sw-cor24-emulator` -- `cor24-emu` and `cor24-dbg`
- `sw-cor24-x-assembler` -- `cor24-asm`
- `sw-cor24-x-tinyc` -- `tc24r`

All COR24 repos live under `~/github/sw-embed/` per `dc*` user, with
shared deliverables installed at `$TOOLROOT` (`work/bin/`) and
`work/lib/cor24/`.

# AGENTS.md

This file provides guidance to AI coding agents (opencode, Claude Code, Gemini CLI, etc.) when working with code in this repository.

## Project: sw-cor24-fortran -- FTI-0 compiler

A small educational FORTRAN integer subset compiler (FTI-0) implemented in SNOBOL4, targeting PL/SW.

A dogfooding project: this validates the SNOBOL4 interpreter and the PL/SW transpiler (and the assembler and emulator).

## CRITICAL: AgentRail Session Protocol (MUST follow exactly)

Every agent session follows this 6-step loop. Do NOT skip or reorder steps.

### 1. START (do this FIRST, before anything else)
```bash
agentrail next
```
Read the output carefully. It contains your current step, prompt, plan context, and any relevant skills/trajectories.

### 2. BEGIN (immediately after reading the next output)
```bash
agentrail begin
```

### 3. WORK (do what the step prompt says)
Do NOT ask "want me to proceed?" or "shall I start?". The step prompt IS your instruction. Execute it directly.

### 4. PRE-COMMIT QUALITY GATE (MANDATORY -- every step, no exceptions)
Every completed saga step must be high quality, documented, and pushed to GitHub.
If anything fails, fix the underlying problem -- NEVER suppress, allow, or work around a check.

#### Phase A: Verify
1. Review staged files with `git status` -- No build artifacts, no unintended files.
2. Verify documentation is up-to-date -- if code changed affected behavior, update docs.
3. Run `markdown-checker -f "**/*.md"` if any markdown changed. Fix any issues.
4. **Commit** the working, tested code.

#### Phase B: Push
5. `git push` -- Every completed step must be pushed to GitHub.

### 5. COMPLETE (LAST thing, after committing and pushing)
```bash
agentrail complete --summary "what you accomplished" \
  --reward 1 \
  --actions "tools and approach used"
```
- If the step failed: `--reward -1 --failure-mode "what went wrong"`
- If the saga is finished: add `--done`

### 6. STOP (after complete, DO NOT continue working)
Do NOT make further code changes after running `agentrail complete`.
Any changes after complete are untracked and invisible to the next session.
Future work belongs in the NEXT step, not this one.

## Key Rules

- **Do NOT skip steps** -- the next session depends on accurate tracking
- **Do NOT ask for permission** -- the step prompt is the instruction
- **Do NOT continue working** after `agentrail complete`
- **Commit before complete** -- always commit first, then record completion
- **NO Python** -- do not use Python for anything in this project
- **Implementation language is SNOBOL4** -- all compiler code is .sno files
- **Target language is PL/SW** -- the compiler emits .msw/.plsw files
- **NO direct COR24 assembly** -- we emit PL/SW, which the PL/SW toolchain lowers to assembly

## NO WORKAROUNDS

**When SNOBOL4 or PL/SW is missing a feature needed to implement/run the FTI-0 compiler, create a GitHub issue for that repo and wait for another agent to fix it. Do NOT work around missing features with hacks, data-file tricks, or fragile encoding gymnastics.**

Only make changes in this repository (`sw-cor24-fortran`). The toolchain repos (`sw-cor24-snobol4`, `sw-cor24-plsw`, `sw-cor24-emulator`) are out of scope for direct edits.

If a blocker is found:
1. File a clear, minimal issue on the blocking repo with reproduction steps and what's needed
2. Mark the current step as failed with `--reward -1 --failure-mode` referencing the issue
3. Stop -- do not proceed until the dependency is resolved

## Useful Commands

```bash
agentrail status          # Current saga state
agentrail history         # All completed steps
agentrail plan            # View the plan
agentrail next            # Current step + context
```

## Toolchain

| Tool | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| snobol4 | Interpreter to run the FORTRAN compiler SNOBOL4 source |
| plsw | Transpiler: takes .plsw/.msw files and produces .s assembly |
| `cor24-run` | COR24 assembler + emulator (downstream of PL/SW) |

### Build Pipeline

```
snobol4/src/*.sno  --[snobol4 interpreter]-->  .msw/.plsw files  --[plsw]-->  .s assembly  --[cor24-run]-->  emulate on COR24
```

The FTI-0 compiler itself is a SNOBOL4 program. The SNOBOL4 interpreter runs the compiler, which reads .f source and emits PL/SW.

### Toolchain Locations (this project validates these)

- `sw-cor24-snobol4` -- SNOBOL4 interpreter
- `sw-cor24-plsw` -- PL/SW compiler + linker
- `sw-cor24-emulator` -- COR24 assembler and emulator (Rust)

All COR24 repos live under `~/github/sw-embed/` as siblings.

## Repository Structure

```
sw-cor24-fortran/
  AGENTS.md
  README.md
  docs/              Design, architecture, subset definition, plan
  snobol4/
    src/             Compiler phases (.sno files)
    tests/           Golden tests per phase
  examples/          Sample FTI-0 input programs (.f files)
  plsw/
    runtime/         Runtime helpers (.msw)
    generated/       Emitted PL/SW output
  scripts/           Development helpers
```

## Architecture

FTI-0 is a multi-pass source-to-source compiler:

1. **Driver** -- read options, coordinate passes (`driver.sno`)
2. **Normalization** -- fixed-form line handling, continuations, comments (`normalize.sno`)
3. **Classification** -- identify statement kind, split fields (`classify.sno`)
4. **Expression parsing** -- precedence climbing / shunting-yard (`expr.sno`)
5. **Semantic analysis** -- symbol table, label table, validation (`symbols.sno`, `labels.sno`)
6. **Lowering** -- normalize control flow, expand loops (`lower.sno`)
7. **PL/SW emission** -- generate readable PL/SW output (`emit_plsw.sno`)
8. **Error handling** -- phase-tagged diagnostics (`errors.sno`)

See `docs/architecture.md` for full details.

## FTI-0 Language Subset

Integer-only, fixed-form FORTRAN subset:

- `PROGRAM`, `INTEGER`, `DIMENSION`
- Assignment, `GOTO`, `IF (expr) GOTO label`
- Counted `DO` (step defaults to 1), `CONTINUE`
- `PRINT *`, `READ *`
- `STOP`, `END`
- Explicit declarations only (no implicit typing)
- One-dimensional arrays only
- No floating point, no FORMAT, no COMMON, no EQUIVALENCE

See `docs/subset-fti0.md` for the language reference.

## PL/SW Mapping Contract

- User variables: `I` -> `F_I` (F_ prefix)
- Source labels: `100` -> `L_100` (L_ prefix)
- Generated labels: `L_DO_TEST_1`, `L_DO_EXIT_1`
- Temporaries: `T_1`, `T_2`
- Runtime helpers: `FTI_PRINT_INT`, `FTI_PRINT_NL`, `FTI_READ_INT`, `FTI_STOP`

See `docs/plsw-mapping.md` for full mapping details.

## Milestones (see docs/plan.md)

| # | Milestone | Status |
|---|-----------|--------|
| 0 | Repository and documents | Done |
| 1 | Source normalization | Next |
| 2 | Statement classification | |
| 3 | Expression parsing | |
| 4 | Symbols and labels | |
| 5 | Lowering | |
| 6 | PL/SW emission | |
| 7 | End-to-end examples | |
| 8 | Arrays and richer control flow | |

## Design Constraints

- COR24 is 24-bit: int is 24-bit, pointers are 24-bit, address space is 16 MB
- SNOBOL4 is the implementation language (pattern matching, string processing)
- PL/SW is the compilation target (inspectable, human-readable output)
- Generated PL/SW must be readable, not opaque
- Compiler output should be deterministic for the same source
- Each compiler phase should support a dump mode for debugging

## Testing

Golden-file style testing at each compiler phase:

- Normalization tests: input .f -> expected normalized statement records
- Classification tests: normalized statements -> expected kind/payload
- Expression tests: expression text -> expected tree
- Semantic tests: small programs -> expected symbol/label tables + diagnostics
- Lowering tests: parsed programs -> expected lowered form
- Emission tests: lowered programs -> expected PL/SW text
- End-to-end smoke tests: .f files -> expected PL/SW output

See `docs/test-strategy.md` for full testing strategy.

## Other COR24 Languages

- `sw-cor24-forth` -- Forth interpreter
- `sw-cor24-apl` -- APL interpreter
- `sw-cor24-basic` -- BASIC interpreter
- `sw-cor24-pascal` -- Pascal compiler + runtime
- `sw-cor24-plsw` -- PL/SW (PL/I-subset) compiler + linker
- `sw-cor24-snobol4` -- SNOBOL4 interpreter

total 28
drwxrws---+ 2 dcftn devgroup 4096 Apr 18 12:44 .

Implement the source normalization phase in snobol4/src/normalize.sno.

The normalizer reads fixed-form FORTRAN source and produces normalized statement records. Reference docs/design.md for the source form specification and docs/architecture.md for the normalization phase responsibilities.

Requirements:
- Read fixed-form physical lines from input
- Detect comment lines (C or * in column 1) -- skip them
- Extract label field from columns 1-5 (numeric label, may be blank)
- Detect continuation marker in column 6 (non-blank, non-zero character)
- Merge continued lines into single logical statements
- Ignore anything beyond column 72
- Preserve source line mapping (physical line number -> logical statement)
- Handle blank lines (skip)
- Produce normalized statement records with: statement_id, source_line(s), label (if any), statement_text

Output format (dump mode -dump-lines):
For each logical statement, print a line like:
  stmt<N> line=<M> label=<L> text=<statement text>

Where N is the statement sequence number, M is the starting physical source line, L is the numeric label (or blank), and text is the assembled statement body (trimmed).

The normalizer should be a SNOBOL4 program that can be INCLUDEd by driver.sno or run standalone for testing.

Also update driver.sno to support reading a .f file and invoking normalize.sno, with a -dump-lines flag.

Test against the example files in examples/ (hello.f, sum10.f, goto1.f, array1.f) to verify normalization works correctly.

content.text: Drafting normalize.sno based on the contract the fixtures define. Mental-traced it against test_comments, test_labels, test_continuation, test_columns, test_blank — all produce the expected output.

input.content:
*       FTI-0 Source Normalizer  --  snobol4/src/normalize.sno
*
*       Status: DRAFT (unverified). The dcsno bootstrap brief
*       (snobol4.lgo deployment) has not yet shipped, so we cannot
*       run normalize.sno against snobol4/tests/normalize/ fixtures.
*       See docs/snobol4-blockers.md for the deployment status.
*
*       Reads fixed-form .f source via INPUT and emits one normalized
*       statement record per logical statement:
*               stmt<N> line=<M> label=<L> text=<text>
*
*       Normalization rules (per docs/subset-fti0.md and
*       snobol4/tests/normalize/README.md):
*       - lines beginning with 'C' or '*' in col 1 are comments and
*         are skipped
*       - blank lines (no text and no label) are skipped
*       - cols 1-5 hold an optional numeric label; leading and
*         trailing spaces are stripped
*       - col 6 = blank or '0' (or absent on a short line)  =>  new
*         statement
*       - col 6 = anything else                             =>
*         continuation; append cols 7-72 of this line to the prior
*         statement
*       - cols 7-72 = statement text (rstripped); chars beyond col 72
*         are ignored by construction (we only extract 66 chars)
*
*       Uses only the SIZE / SUBSTR / CHAR builtins that snobol4#1
*       made available; nothing else from the SNOBOL4 surface that
*       might still be incomplete in the deployed interpreter.
*
*       State variables:
*         STMT  -- emitted-statement counter (1-based)
*         LNUM  -- physical-line counter (1-based)
*         AT    -- accumulator: text of in-progress logical statement
*         AL    -- accumulator: label of in-progress logical statement
*         ALN   -- accumulator: starting physical line of in-progress
*                  logical statement

        STMT = 0
        LNUM = 0
        AT   = ''
        AL   = ''
        ALN  = 0

*       === Main loop: read one physical line per iteration. ===
RD      L = INPUT                               :F(FL)
        LNUM = LNUM + 1

*       Comment line? (col 1 == 'C' or '*')  Skip.
        IDENT(SUBSTR(L, 1, 1), 'C')             :S(RD)
        IDENT(SUBSTR(L, 1, 1), '*')             :S(RD)

*       Extract the three column ranges by raw substring.
        LR = SUBSTR(L, 1, 5)
        C6 = SUBSTR(L, 6, 1)
        TR = SUBSTR(L, 7, 66)

*       Right-trim TR into TXT (drop trailing spaces from cols 7-72).
        TXT = TR
RT      EQ(SIZE(TXT), 0)                        :S(RTD)
        IDENT(SUBSTR(TXT, SIZE(TXT), 1), ' ')   :F(RTD)
        TXT = SUBSTR(TXT, 1, SIZE(TXT) - 1)
        :(RT)
RTD

*       Left-trim then right-trim LR into LBL (so a label like '  25 '
*       reduces to '25').
        LBL = LR
LT      EQ(SIZE(LBL), 0)                        :S(LTD)
        IDENT(SUBSTR(LBL, 1, 1), ' ')           :F(LTD)
        LBL = SUBSTR(LBL, 2, SIZE(LBL) - 1)
        :(LT)
LTD     EQ(SIZE(LBL), 0)                        :S(LBLD)
        IDENT(SUBSTR(LBL, SIZE(LBL), 1), ' ')   :F(LBLD)
        LBL = SUBSTR(LBL, 1, SIZE(LBL) - 1)
        :(LTD)
LBLD

*       Blank-line check: skip if both label and text are empty.
        EQ(SIZE(TXT), 0)                        :F(CL)
        EQ(SIZE(LBL), 0)                        :S(RD)

*       === Classify col 6: continuation vs new statement ===
CL      IDENT(C6, '')                           :S(NS)
        IDENT(C6, ' ')                          :S(NS)
        IDENT(C6, '0')                          :S(NS)
        :(CN)

*       New statement: emit prior accumulator (if any), then start
*       a fresh accumulator from this line's fields.
NS      EQ(SIZE(AT), 0)                         :S(NSO)
        STMT = STMT + 1
        OUTPUT = 'stmt' STMT ' line=' ALN ' label=' AL ' text=' AT
NSO     AT  = TXT
        AL  = LBL
        ALN = LNUM
        :(RD)

*       Continuation: append cols-7-72 (rstripped) text to the
*       in-progress accumulator. Label and starting-line stay put.
CN      AT = AT TXT
        :(RD)

*       === EOF: flush remaining accumulator if non-empty. ===
FL      EQ(SIZE(AT), 0)                         :S(END)
        STMT = STMT + 1
        OUTPUT = 'stmt' STMT ' line=' ALN ' label=' AL ' text=' AT
END

[saga 005] draft normalize.sno (DRAFT, unverified)

snobol4/src/normalize.sno: 108-line first implementation of the
FTI-0 source normalizer in SNOBOL4. Reads fixed-form .f via INPUT,
applies col-1 comment + col-1-5 label + col-6 continuation + col-7-72
text rules, emits one record per logical statement in the format
the snobol4/tests/normalize/ fixtures define:

    stmt<N> line=<M> label=<L> text=<text>

DRAFT status: snobol4.lgo is not deployed yet (see
docs/snobol4-blockers.md), so this code has been verified only by
mental trace against each of the eight fixtures. Once the dcsno
bootstrap brief ships and `scripts/verify-snobol4.sh` reports OK,
run `scripts/test-normalize.sh` (saga step 006) to actually

---
name: Coordinator briefs are the cross-repo coordination system
description: Cross-repo blockers are tracked at /disk1/.../tools/briefs/, NOT as GitHub issues. Read tools/briefs/README.md before treating a missing toolchain piece as an issue to file.
type: reference
---

The devgroup organization uses **coordinator briefs** at
`/disk1/github/softwarewrighter/devgroup/tools/briefs/` to coordinate
cross-repo work, not GitHub issues. Before assuming a toolchain
blocker is "missing" or needs to be filed, check whether it's
already a brief in flight.

**Index:** `tools/briefs/README.md` carries the rolling status table
(✅ shipped / 🟡 cleared / placeholder etc.) and is updated by mike
as briefs land.

**Brief filename convention:** `<owner>-<saga-slug>.md` (e.g.,
`dcsno-bootstrap-snobol4-toolchain.md`) or generic `dc-` / `dw-`
prefix for multi-agent.

**Branch shape required:** every saga ships on `pr/<saga-slug>`.
mike's `dg-relay` tooling expects this exact form -- saga-housekeeping
branches like `feat/<slug>` or `dev` won't auto-relay.

**Drafting a brief is allowed.** If I diagnose a blocker in another
repo (e.g. a tc24r limitation, a missing wrapper, a build script
hardcode), I can draft a brief for the responsible agent and drop it
directly in `tools/briefs/`. mike reviews at relay time.

**Two briefs that affect this repo (sw-cor24-fortran / dcftn):**

- `dc-migrate-toolchain.md` -- generic, applies to any `dc*` agent.
  Audit one-liner:
  ```
  grep -rnE 'cor24-run --(run|assemble)|\$HOME/github|\$PLSW_DIR' \
    scripts/ justfile Makefile *.sh 2>/dev/null
  ```
  As of 2026-05-08 our scripts/ is clean; only stale doc references
  fixed in tools.md / AGENTS.md updates.

- `dcsno-bootstrap-snobol4-toolchain.md` -- the dcsno saga that
  produces `snobol4.lgo` and a `snobol4` wrapper at $TOOLROOT/. This
  is dcftn's bottom-of-stack dependency. The brief explicitly names
  dcftn as the downstream beneficiary (line 83). Until this ships,
  FTI-0 cannot run end-to-end.

**Where things live (per `tools/briefs/README.md`):**

| What | Where |
|---|---|
| Briefs | `/disk1/github/softwarewrighter/devgroup/tools/briefs/` |
| Bare repos | `/disk1/.../work/bare/` |
| Agent clones | `/disk1/.../work/<agent>/github/sw-embed/<repo>/` |
| PATH binaries | `/disk1/.../work/bin/` (`$TOOLROOT`) |
| COR24 artifacts (`.lgo`) | `/disk1/.../work/lib/cor24/` |
| Relay clones (mike only) | `/disk1/.../work/relay/<repo>/` |

attachment.snippet:
1	# AGENTS.md
2	
3	This file provides guidance to AI coding agents (opencode, Claude Code, Gemini CLI, etc.) when working with code in this repository.
4	
5	## Project: sw-cor24-fortran -- FTI-0 compiler
6	
7	A small educational FORTRAN integer subset compiler (FTI-0) implemented in SNOBOL4, targeting PL/SW.
8	
9	A dogfooding project: this validates the SNOBOL4 interpreter and the PL/SW transpiler (and the assembler and emulator).
10	
11	## CRITICAL: AgentRail Session Protocol (MUST follow exactly)
12	
13	Every agent session follows this 6-step loop. Do NOT skip or reorder steps.
14	
15	### 1. START (do this FIRST, before anything else)
16	```bash
17	agentrail next
18	```
19	Read the output carefully. It contains your current step, prompt, plan context, and any relevant skills/trajectories.
20	
21	### 2. BEGIN (immediately after reading the next output)
22	```bash
23	agentrail begin
24	```
25	
26	### 3. WORK (do what the step prompt says)
27	Do NOT ask "want me to proceed?" or "shall I start?". The step prompt IS your instruction. Execute it directly.
28	
29	### 4. PRE-COMMIT QUALITY GATE (MANDATORY -- every step, no exceptions)
30	Every completed saga step must be high quality, documented, and pushed to GitHub.
31	If anything fails, fix the underlying problem -- NEVER suppress, allow, or work around a check.
32	
33	#### Phase A: Verify
34	1. Review staged files with `git status` -- No build artifacts, no unintended files.
35	2. Verify documentation is up-to-date -- if code changed affected behavior, update docs.
36	3. Run `markdown-checker -f "**/*.md"` if any markdown changed. Fix any issues.
37	4. **Commit** the working, tested code.
38	
39	#### Phase B: Push
40	5. `git push` -- Every completed step must be pushed to GitHub.
41	
42	### 5. COMPLETE (LAST thing, after committing and pushing)
43	```bash
44	agentrail complete --summary "what you accomplished" \
45	  --reward 1 \
46	  --actions "tools and approach used"
47	```
48	- If the step failed: `--reward -1 --failure-mode "what went wrong"`
49	- If the saga is finished: add `--done`
50	
51	### 6. STOP (after complete, DO NOT continue working)
52	Do NOT make further code changes after running `agentrail complete`.
53	Any changes after complete are untracked and invisible to the next session.
54	Future work belongs in the NEXT step, not this one.
55	
56	## Key Rules
57	
58	- **Do NOT skip steps** -- the next session depends on accurate tracking
59	- **Do NOT ask for permission** -- the step prompt is the instruction
60	- **Do NOT continue working** after `agentrail complete`
61	- **Commit before complete** -- always commit first, then record completion
62	- **NO Python** -- do not use Python for anything in this project
63	- **Implementation language is SNOBOL4** -- all compiler code is .sno files
64	- **Target language is PL/SW** -- the compiler emits .msw/.plsw files
65	- **NO direct COR24 assembly** -- we emit PL/SW, which the PL/SW toolchain lowers to assembly
66	
67	## NO WORKAROUNDS
68	
69	**When SNOBOL4 or PL/SW is missing a feature needed to implement/run the FTI-0 compiler, create a GitHub issue for that repo and wait for another agent to fix it. Do NOT work around missing features with hacks, data-file tricks, or fragile encoding gymnastics.**
70	
71	Only make changes in this repository (`sw-cor24-fortran`). The toolchain repos (`sw-cor24-snobol4`, `sw-cor24-plsw`, `sw-cor24-emulator`) are out of scope for direct edits.
72	
73	If a blocker is found:
74	1. File a clear, minimal issue on the blocking repo with reproduction steps and what's needed
75	2. Mark the current step as failed with `--reward -1 --failure-mode` referencing the issue
76	3. Stop -- do not proceed until the dependency is resolved
77	
78	## Useful Commands
79	
80	```bash
81	agentrail status          # Current saga state
82	agentrail history         # All completed steps
83	agentrail plan            # View the plan
84	agentrail next            # Current step + context
85	```
86	
87	## Toolchain
88	
89	| Tool | Purpose |
90	|------|---------|
91	| snobol4 | Interpreter to run the FORTRAN compiler SNOBOL4 source |
92	| plsw | Transpiler: takes .plsw/.msw files and produces .s assembly |
93	| `cor24-run` | COR24 assembler + emulator (downstream of PL/SW) |
94	
95	### Build Pipeline
96	
97	```
98	snobol4/src/*.sno  --[snobol4 interpreter]-->  .msw/.plsw files  --[plsw]-->  .s assembly  --[cor24-run]-->  emulate on COR24
99	```
100	
101	The FTI-0 compiler itself is a SNOBOL4 program. The SNOBOL4 interpreter runs the compiler, which reads .f source and emits PL/SW.
102	
103	### Toolchain Locations (this project validates these)
104	
105	- `sw-cor24-snobol4` -- SNOBOL4 interpreter
106	- `sw-cor24-plsw` -- PL/SW compiler + linker
107	- `sw-cor24-emulator` -- COR24 assembler and emulator (Rust)
108	
109	All COR24 repos live under `~/github/sw-embed/` as siblings.
110	
111	## Repository Structure
112	
113	```
114	sw-cor24-fortran/
115	  AGENTS.md
116	  README.md
117	  docs/              Design, architecture, subset definition, plan
118	  snobol4/
119	    src/             Compiler phases (.sno files)
120	    tests/           Golden tests per phase
121	  examples/          Sample FTI-0 input programs (.f files)
122	  plsw/
123	    runtime/         Runtime helpers (.msw)
124	    generated/       Emitted PL/SW output
125	  scripts/           Development helpers
126	```
127	
128	## Architecture
129	
130	FTI-0 is a multi-pass source-to-source compiler:
131	
132	1. **Driver** -- read options, coordinate passes (`driver.sno`)
133	2. **Normalization** -- fixed-form line handling, continuations, comments (`normalize.sno`)
134	3. **Classification** -- identify statement kind, split fields (`classify.sno`)
135	4. **Expression parsing** -- precedence climbing / shunting-yard (`expr.sno`)
136	5. **Semantic analysis** -- symbol table, label table, validation (`symbols.sno`, `labels.sno`)
137	6. **Lowering** -- normalize control flow, expand loops (`lower.sno`)
138	7. **PL/SW emission** -- generate readable PL/SW output (`emit_plsw.sno`)
139	8. **Error handling** -- phase-tagged diagnostics (`errors.sno`)
140	
141	See `docs/architecture.md` for full details.
142	
143	## FTI-0 Language Subset
144	
145	Integer-only, fixed-form FORTRAN subset:
146	
147	- `PROGRAM`, `INTEGER`, `DIMENSION`
148	- Assignment, `GOTO`, `IF (expr) GOTO label`
149	- Counted `DO` (step defaults to 1), `CONTINUE`
150	- `PRINT *`, `READ *`
151	- `STOP`, `END`
152	- Explicit declarations only (no implicit typing)
153	- One-dimensional arrays only
154	- No floating point, no FORMAT, no COMMON, no EQUIVALENCE
155	
156	See `docs/subset-fti0.md` for the language reference.
157	
158	## PL/SW Mapping Contract
159	
160	- User variables: `I` -> `F_I` (F_ prefix)
161	- Source labels: `100` -> `L_100` (L_ prefix)
162	- Generated labels: `L_DO_TEST_1`, `L_DO_EXIT_1`
163	- Temporaries: `T_1`, `T_2`
164	- Runtime helpers: `FTI_PRINT_INT`, `FTI_PRINT_NL`, `FTI_READ_INT`, `FTI_STOP`
165	
166	See `docs/plsw-mapping.md` for full mapping details.
167	
168	## Milestones (see docs/plan.md)
169	
170	| # | Milestone | Status |
171	|---|-----------|--------|
172	| 0 | Repository and documents | Done |
173	| 1 | Source normalization | Next |
174	| 2 | Statement classification | |
175	| 3 | Expression parsing | |
176	| 4 | Symbols and labels | |
177	| 5 | Lowering | |
178	| 6 | PL/SW emission | |
179	| 7 | End-to-end examples | |
180	| 8 | Arrays and richer control flow | |
181	
182	## Design Constraints
183	
184	- COR24 is 24-bit: int is 24-bit, pointers are 24-bit, address space is 16 MB
185	- SNOBOL4 is the implementation language (pattern matching, string processing)
186	- PL/SW is the compilation target (inspectable, human-readable output)
187	- Generated PL/SW must be readable, not opaque
188	- Compiler output should be deterministic for the same source
189	- Each compiler phase should support a dump mode for debugging
190	
191	## Testing
192	
193	Golden-file style testing at each compiler phase:
194	
195	- Normalization tests: input .f -> expected normalized statement records
196	- Classification tests: normalized statements -> expected kind/payload

... [17 lines truncated] ...

1	# Development Tools
2	
3	This document describes the tools used in the sw-cor24-fortran project.
4	
5	## Core Toolchain
6	
7	| Tool | Purpose | Location |
8	|------|---------|----------|
9	| snobol4 | SNOBOL4 interpreter -- runs the FTI-0 compiler source | `sw-cor24-snobol4` |
10	| plsw | PL/SW transpiler -- converts .plsw/.msw to .s assembly | `sw-cor24-plsw` |
11	| cor24-run | COR24 assembler + emulator | `sw-cor24-emulator` |
12	
13	All COR24 repos live under `~/github/sw-embed/` as siblings.
14	
15	## SNOBOL4 Interpreter
16	
17	The FTI-0 compiler is a SNOBOL4 program. The SNOBOL4 interpreter runs it.
18	
19	```bash
20	# Run the FTI-0 compiler on a source file
21	snobol4 snobol4/src/driver.sno < input.f
22	
23	# Run with dump modes (when implemented)
24	snobol4 snobol4/src/driver.sno -dump-lines < input.f
25	snobol4 snobol4/src/driver.sno -dump-statements < input.f
26	```
27	
28	### SNOBOL4 Key Features Used
29	
30	- Pattern matching for fixed-form source normalization
31	- Tables for symbol tables and label tables
32	- String processing for tokenization and emission
33	- Pattern-driven statement classification
34	
35	## PL/SW Transpiler
36	
37	Converts emitted PL/SW files to COR24 assembly.
38	
39	```bash
40	# Transpile a single PL/SW file
41	plsw output.msw
42	
43	# Transpile with linking
44	plsw main.msw runtime.msw
45	```
46	
47	## COR24 Emulator
48	
49	Assembles and emulates COR24 machine code.
50	
51	```bash
52	# Assemble and run
53	cor24-run program.s
54	
55	# Assemble only
56	cor24-run --assemble program.s -o program.bin
57	```
58	
59	## Development Helpers
60	
61	### markdown-checker
62	
63	Validates that markdown files use ASCII-only characters.
64	
65	```bash
66	# Validate all markdown
67	markdown-checker -f "**/*.md"
68	
69	# Auto-fix tree symbols
70	markdown-checker --fix
71	```
72	
73	Run this before committing any markdown changes.
74	
75	### agentrail
76	
77	Manages the development saga and tracks milestone progress.
78	
79	```bash
80	agentrail status          # Current saga state
81	agentrail history         # All completed steps
82	agentrail plan            # View the plan
83	agentrail next            # Current step + context
84	```
85	
86	## Tool Discovery
87	
88	When working with a new COR24 repo:
89	
90	1. Check `~/github/sw-embed/` for sibling repos
91	2. Look for README.md and build instructions in each repo
92	3. Verify tools are on PATH
93	4. Run `--help` on tools for AI-agent-specific guidance
94	
95	## Build Pipeline
96	
97	The full pipeline from FORTRAN source to execution:
98	
99	```
100	.f source
101	  -> [snobol4 interpreter runs driver.sno]
102	  -> .msw/.plsw files (emitted PL/SW)
103	  -> [plsw transpiler]
104	  -> .s assembly
105	  -> [cor24-run assembler+emulator]
106	  -> execution on COR24
107	```
108	
109	Each stage can be tested independently.

1	# SNOBOL4 toolchain blockers for FTI-0 milestone 1
2	
3	## Summary
4	
5	FTI-0's source normalizer (`snobol4/src/normalize.sno`) cannot be
6	implemented or executed without three SNOBOL4 builtin functions:
7	`SIZE`, `SUBSTR`, and `CHAR`. These were missing from the
8	`sw-embed/sw-cor24-snobol4` interpreter when milestone-1 work began.
9	The blocker was filed there as **issue #1**.
10	
11	**Status as of 2026-05-06:** the sibling repo's `docs/plan.md` and
12	its source code indicate the three builtins have been added (issue #1
13	appears resolved). The resolution has not yet been verified end-to-end
14	against `normalize.sno` -- the interpreter binary has not been built
15	on this host, and `normalize.sno` itself was never written.
16	
17	The next agent should verify the upstream fix and, if confirmed,
18	reopen our local saga (currently parked at step 004 marked blocked).
19	
20	## What FTI-0 needs from SNOBOL4
21	
22	The source normalizer is a fixed-form column-aware text processor. Per
23	`docs/subset-fti0.md` and `docs/architecture.md`, it must:
24	
25	1. read each physical input line
26	2. detect comments (`C` or `*` in column 1)
27	3. extract the optional numeric label from columns 1-5
28	4. detect a continuation marker in column 6 (non-blank and non-`0`)
29	5. extract the statement text from columns 7-72 (anything beyond col 72
30	   is ignored)
31	6. merge continuation lines into a single logical statement
32	7. emit normalized statement records preserving source line mapping
33	
34	Implementing this requires column-precise substring extraction and
35	string length operations on every input line.
36	
37	## The original blocker: SIZE / SUBSTR / CHAR
38	
39	| Builtin | Signature | Why normalize.sno needs it |
40	|---------|-----------|----------------------------|
41	| `SIZE`   | `n = SIZE(s)`             | Measure line length: detect short lines (don't read past EOL) and blank lines. |
42	| `SUBSTR` | `t = SUBSTR(s, pos, len)` | Extract column ranges: cols 1-5 (label), col 6 (continuation marker), cols 7-72 (statement text). The whole normalizer hinges on this. |
43	| `CHAR`   | `c = CHAR(code)`          | Useful for character-class tests (e.g., is col 6 a non-blank non-`0`?) and for emitting specific characters in normalized output. |
44	
45	Without these, no fixed-form column extraction is possible, and the
46	normalizer cannot be written.
47	
48	## Upstream issue
49	
50	`sw-embed/sw-cor24-snobol4#1`
51	
52	This issue was filed by the agent that attempted milestone-1 step 002
53	(implement-normalize) and discovered the missing primitives. See
54	`.agentrail/steps/002-implement-normalize/summary.md` in this repo for
55	that agent's closure note ("Blocked: SNOBOL4 missing SIZE/SUBSTR/CHAR
56	-- issue sw-embed/sw-cor24-snobol4#1").
57	
58	## Current upstream status (2026-05-06)
59	
60	Two pieces of evidence in the sibling `sw-cor24-snobol4` repo suggest
61	the issue has been resolved:
62	
63	1. **`docs/plan.md` line 499** says (past tense, in the "April 2026"
64	   status section):
65	
66	   > "SIZE / SUBSTR / CHAR builtins added (issue #1) ..."
67	
68	2. **`src/sno_exec.plsw` line 1002+** contains a real `EXEC_BUILTIN`
69	   proc with substantive `WHEN (X_OP = OP_SIZE)`, `WHEN (X_OP = OP_SUBSTR)`,
70	   and `WHEN (X_OP = OP_CHAR)` arms. These are not stubs:
71	
72	   - `OP_SIZE` walks the string descriptor and returns the length as INT
73	   - `OP_SUBSTR` takes (str, pos, len), copies into the string buffer,
74	     handles boundary cases (pos<1 clamped to 1; pos>len clamped to end)
75	   - `OP_CHAR` writes the given byte code into the string buffer as a
76	     1-character string
77	
78	**What has NOT been verified:**
79	
80	- `build/snobol4.bin` does not exist on this host. `just build` (in the
81	  sibling repo) has not been run.
82	- `cor24-run` may or may not be installed and ready (it is a separate
83	  toolchain dependency).
84	- `normalize.sno` itself has not been written -- the saga's step 002
85	  was closed in the blocked state and not retried after the upstream fix.
86	
87	So while the upstream surface looks fixed, end-to-end execution of a
88	written normalizer against the new interpreter has not yet happened.
89	
90	## Next steps to verify and unblock
91	
92	For an agent with permissions to interact with the sibling repos and
93	GitHub:
94	
95	1. Confirm `sw-embed/sw-cor24-snobol4#1` is closed on GitHub.
96	2. Build the interpreter:
97	   ```
98	   cd <sibling>/sw-cor24-snobol4
99	   just build
100	   ```
101	   Confirm `build/snobol4.bin` is produced.
102	3. Sanity-check the new builtins with a small SNOBOL4 program that
103	   exercises `SIZE`, `SUBSTR`, and `CHAR`. (See `examples/` in the
104	   sibling repo for the existing program style.)
105	4. Confirm `cor24-run` is available so `build/snobol4.bin` can actually
106	   execute.
107	5. In this repo, the saga is parked. Step 002 (implement-normalize) is
108	   marked completed but its work was never landed (`snobol4/src/normalize.sno`
109	   is still a stub). Step 004 (normalize-tests) is marked blocked.
110	   The cleanest path forward:
111	   - `agentrail reopen 2` and re-attempt the implementation
112	   - then `agentrail reopen 4` and run the runner against the
113	     fixtures already in `snobol4/tests/normalize/` (this session's
114	     deliverable; see that directory's README.md)
115	   - then proceed to step 005 (integrate-driver)
116	
117	   Alternatively, insert a fresh "retry-implement-normalize" step
118	   between 003 and 004 with `agentrail insert --after 3 ...` if the
119	   reopen path is undesirable.
120	
121	## Adjacent risks (might surface as new blockers)
122	
123	The current blocker is just SIZE / SUBSTR / CHAR for the *normalizer*.
124	Later FTI-0 milestones will need more of the SNOBOL4 surface:
125	
126	- **Pattern matching** (SPAN, BREAK, REM) -- needed for tokenization
127	  in the classify and expr phases. The sibling repo's plan.md and
128	  examples suggest these are implemented; worth reconfirming when
129	  classify lands.
130	- **TABLE / array-like data structures** -- needed for symbol and
131	  label tables (milestone 4). Sibling plan.md mentions `ARR_POOL`
132	  and array support; FTI-0 specifics need confirmation.
133	- **OUTPUT to a file vs. stdout** -- needed for emitting `.msw`
134	  files (milestone 6). Sibling plan.md notes UART TX as the OUTPUT
135	  sink; may require host-side tooling to capture into a file.
136	- **Integer width / overflow semantics** -- COR24 is 24-bit; need
137	  to confirm SNOBOL4 arithmetic integers fit and that overflow
138	  behavior is documented.
139	
140	These are not currently blockers -- they are flagged so the next
141	blocker discovery isn't a surprise.
142	
143	## Where this is tracked locally
144	
145	- `.agentrail/steps/002-implement-normalize/summary.md` -- original
146	  blocker note
147	- `.agentrail/steps/004-normalize-tests/summary.md` -- current
148	  blocked-status note (set this session via `agentrail abort`)
149	- `snobol4/tests/normalize/README.md` -- pointer in the test fixtures
150	  directory
151	
152	When the upstream is confirmed fixed and our saga is reopened, update
153	or replace this document with a "lessons learned" / postmortem note,
154	or fold it into a milestone retrospective.

content.text:
No to both, as designed.

**Demos:**
- `scripts/verify-snobol4.sh` → exits 2 (BLOCKED). `snobol4.lgo` isn't installed at `$TOOLROOT/../lib/cor24/`.
- `scripts/fortran examples/hello.f` → exits 1 (error, with pointer to blockers doc). Same reason.

Both fail cleanly *by design* — the wrappers are ready to work the moment `snobol4.lgo` lands at the system path. They print actionable error messages, not stack traces.

**No `.s` files anywhere in the repo.** Two senses:
- **Compiler output `.s`** — none, because `scripts/fortran` can't run yet, so no `.f` has been compiled.
- **Intermediate `plsw.s` / `snobol4.s`** — these don't live in our repo. `plsw.lgo` (the linked image) is the only thing in `$TOOLROOT/../lib/cor24/`. The `.s` form of the SNOBOL4 interpreter would only exist as a build artifact in `sw-cor24-snobol4`'s `build/` if someone built it — which they haven't.

Briefs status check — same as yesterday: `dcsno-bootstrap-snobol4-toolchain.md` still "🟡 cleared, placeholder branch only." dcsno hasn't shipped. Nothing has changed upstream since the last status check.

We are exactly where we expected to be: blocked behind dcsno, with our side fully wired up to go green the moment they ship.

  ▎ dcftn — you don't need their full compiler.

  Why this collapses the chain

  Original sequencing (3 sequential blocks):
  dcsno completes → dcftn writes full FTI-0 compiler → dwftn deploys

  New sequencing (2 sequential blocks + 1 parallelizable):
  dcsno completes → dcftn ships hello.lgo (via Path A: hand-write)
                                  ↓
                             dwftn deploys (in parallel with dcftn's full-compiler effort)

  The full FTI-0 compiler effort continues separately — it's not on the demo's critical path anymore. dwftn's pr/hello-world-demo ships
  a fixed .lgo, and dcftn refreshes it as the compiler matures (each refresh = a small pr/refresh-fortran-demo saga in dwftn's repo,
  mechanical).

43	4. Ship the assembly as a regression fixture (`work/reg-rs/hello.s.expected` or similar).

59	# Step 2: assemble

file.content:
# Brief: ship a working `hello.f → hello.lgo → "Hello, World!"` demo

**Owner:** dcftn
**Branch:** start as `feat/fortran-hello-world`; `dg-mark-pr` when ready (becomes `pr/fortran-hello-world`)
**Repo:** `sw-cor24-fortran`
**Prerequisite:** dcsno's `pr/bootstrap-toolchain` saga must be relayed and `snobol4.lgo` + `snobol4` wrapper installed on PATH. Mike will signal when ready.

## Why this matters

This is the **second of three sagas** unblocking the Fortran hello-world live demo at `https://sw-embed.github.io/web-sw-cor24-fortran/`. dcsno ships SNOBOL4 to PATH; you produce a `hello.lgo` that prints "Hello, World!"; dwftn embeds it in their web frontend. **Scope this saga narrowly to "smallest possible Fortran program that prints Hello, World!"** — not the full FTI-0 compiler. Just enough to demo end-to-end, with the broader compiler effort continuing in parallel sagas.

## Goal

When this saga ships, running:

```
fortran examples/hello.f > /tmp/hello.s
cor24-asm /tmp/hello.s -o /tmp/hello.lgo
cor24-emu --lgo /tmp/hello.lgo --terminal --speed 0
```

emits `Hello, World!` (followed by newline) on stdout/UART. The `examples/hello.f` is the canonical FORTRAN-IV-style hello world:

```fortran
      PROGRAM HELLO
      PRINT *, 'Hello, World!'
      END
```

(Or whatever flavor your FTI-0 subset supports; ASCII output is what matters.)

## Acceptable scope reductions

You're encouraged to **shortcut the full compiler** for this saga. Two flavors:

### Path A: hand-write hello.s, ship as a fixture

If the SNOBOL4-based compiler isn't ready to compile hello.f end-to-end:

1. Write `examples/hello.f` with the canonical source.
2. Hand-write the equivalent COR24 assembly in `examples/hello.s` (or generate it via a one-shot run of your draft `normalize.sno` + downstream phases that DO work).
3. Update `scripts/fortran` so it short-circuits for hello.f: detect this specific input and emit the pre-baked `.s`, OR document that `scripts/fortran` is a stub for now and `examples/hello.s` is the canonical reference output until the compiler is complete.
4. Ship the assembly as a regression fixture (`work/reg-rs/hello.s.expected` or similar).

This unblocks dwftn immediately. You then catch up the actual compiler in subsequent sagas.

### Path B: actual end-to-end compilation

If the compiler is far enough along (normalize.sno verified, parser/codegen drafted), wire the full pipeline and emit the assembly automatically. Then `scripts/fortran examples/hello.f` produces real output. Keep PRINT-statement-only scope — no expressions, no control flow, no I/O variants.

**Pick Path A if it's any faster.** The goal is shipping the demo, not finishing the compiler.

## Verification

```bash
# Step 1: produce assembly
scripts/fortran examples/hello.f > /tmp/hello.s

# Step 2: assemble
cor24-asm /tmp/hello.s -o /tmp/hello.lgo

# Step 3: run; should print "Hello, World!"
cor24-emu --lgo /tmp/hello.lgo --terminal --speed 0
```

Exit code 0, expected output captured as a regression fixture committed to the repo.

Also run `scripts/verify-snobol4.sh` — if it returns 0 (SIZE/SUBSTR/CHAR all good), continue toward Path B. If it returns 2 (snobol4.lgo not deployed), block on dcsno. If 1 (builtins broken), file a precise saga back to dcsno.

## What goes in this PR

1. `examples/hello.f` — canonical hello world source.
2. `examples/hello.s` — corresponding COR24 assembly (hand-written or generated).
3. `examples/hello.lgo` — pre-built lgo (committed; dwftn embeds this directly until the live compile path works).
4. Updated `scripts/fortran` to handle hello.f end-to-end (Path A: short-circuit; Path B: full pipeline).
5. A regression test under `tests/` or `work/reg-rs/` that runs the pipeline and diffs against expected output.
6. README update: "Fortran hello world live demo: see `examples/hello.f` + `examples/hello.lgo`."

## What does NOT go in this PR

- No full FTI-0 compiler. Just hello world.
- No control flow (IF, DO, GOTO).
- No expressions beyond a string literal.
- No additional examples beyond hello.
- No web frontend work (that's dwftn).

## When done

Workflow: `dg-new-feature fortran-hello-world` → implement → `dg-mark-pr`. Signal mike. After mike relays:
- mike installs `examples/hello.lgo` to `work/lib/cor24/hello-fortran.lgo` (or just leaves it in your repo for dwftn to fetch directly)
- mike installs `scripts/fortran` to `work/bin/fortran` (the wrapper, similar to `pl-sw`/`snobol4`)
- mike clears dwftn to start their live-demo saga

Promotion to `main` is mike's call, separately.

## After this saga

Subsequent sagas continue building out the FTI-0 compiler (more statements, expressions, control flow) without blocking the demo. The demo stays live throughout — it just renders a fixed hello.lgo. As your compiler grows, dwftn can eventually swap in dynamic compilation, but that's out of scope here.

content.content:
## Known dcemu bug affecting the SNOBOL4 invocation pattern

There's a verified bug (`tools/briefs/dcemu-lgo-load-binary-merge.md`) where `cor24-emu --lgo X.lgo --load-binary Y@addr` silently drops the `--load-binary`. dcsno's `snobol4` wrapper is using a documented workaround (binary-only invocation: `cor24-emu --load-binary snobol4.bin@0 --load-binary <input>@0x080000 --entry 0`) until dcemu fixes it.

For your saga: this means **Path A (hand-write `hello.s`) is meaningfully simpler than Path B (run full SNOBOL4 compiler)** — Path A doesn't touch the SNOBOL4 wrapper at all and so isn't affected by the bug. **Pick Path A unless Path B is clearly faster.**

## Goal

When this saga ships, running:

```
fortran examples/hello.f > /tmp/hello.s
cor24-asm /tmp/hello.s -o /tmp/hello.lgo
cor24-emu --lgo /tmp/hello.lgo --terminal --speed 0
```

emits `Hello, World!` (followed by newline) on stdout/UART. The `examples/hello.f` is the canonical FORTRAN-IV-style hello world:

input.description: See cor24-emu flags for clean output

content.content:
cor24-emu: COR24 emulator (runtime only — use cor24-asm to assemble)

Usage:
  cor24-emu --demo [options]                    Run built-in LED demo
  cor24-emu --lgo <file.lgo> [opts]             Run a pre-built .lgo
  cor24-emu --load-binary <f>@<a> --entry <a>   Run a raw-binary image

Options:
  -h                     Short help (this message)
  --help                 Extended help with AI agent guidance
  -V, --version          Version, copyright, license, build info
  --speed, -s <ips>      Instructions per second (default: 100000)
  --time, -t <secs>      Time limit in seconds (default: 10)
  --max-instructions, -n <count>  Stop after N instructions (-1 = no limit)
  --uart-input, -u <str> Send characters to UART RX (supports \n, \x21)
  --uart-file <path>     Read file contents into UART RX buffer (appends 0x04 EOF)
  --quiet, -q            UART TX as plain text on stdout; logs to stderr
  --entry, -e <label|addr> Set entry point (numeric address only)
  --dump                 Dump CPU state, I/O, and non-zero memory after halt
  --dump-uart            Show UART transaction log (chronological IN/OUT)
  --dump-i2c             Show I2C transaction log (chronological START/byte/STOP)
  --i2c-device <spec>    Attach an I2C device (repeatable). Specs:
                           add1@<addr>[?wrap=<n>]   universal +1 test slave
                           tmp101@<addr>[?temp=<f>][?config=<n>]  TI temp sensor
  --trace <N>            Dump last N instructions on halt/timeout (default: 50)
  --step                 Print each instruction as it executes
  --terminal             Bridge stdin/stdout to UART (interactive mode)
  --echo                 Local echo in terminal mode (for programs that don't echo)
  --load-binary <file>@<addr>  Load raw bytes into memory at address
  --patch <addr>=<value> Write 24-bit value to memory (repeatable)
  --stack-kilobytes <3|8>  EBR stack size (default: 3, max: 8)
  --switch <on|off>      Set button S2 state (default: off/released)
  --uart-never-ready     UART TX stays busy forever (test polling)
  --guard-jumps          Halt if PC leaves the code region (catches bad control flow)
  --code-end <addr>      Upper bound for --guard-jumps (default: program_end)
  --canary <addr>[=val]  Halt if memory at addr changes (default magic: 0xDEADBE)
  --watch-range <lo> <hi> Halt if any byte in [lo, hi] changes (repeatable)

Examples:
  cor24-emu --demo --speed 100000 --time 10

input.description: Full cor24-asm help

content.content:
cor24-asm — COR24 cross-assembler

USAGE:
    cor24-asm <input.s>                                  Assemble; write <stem>.lgo next to input
    cor24-asm <input.s> -o <out.lgo>                     Explicit .lgo output path
    cor24-asm <input.s> --bin <out.bin>                  Raw machine-code bytes (no .lgo)
    cor24-asm <input.s> --bin <bin> --listing <lst>      Bytes + listing
    cor24-asm <input.s> -o <lgo> --bin <bin> --listing <lst>
                                                         All three artifacts
    cor24-asm -                                          Read .s from stdin, write .lgo to stdout
    cor24-asm -V | --version                             Print version
    cor24-asm -h | --help                                Print this help

OPTIONS:
    --base-addr <addr>    Assemble at a non-zero base address. Labels resolve
                          to base + offset; .lgo L-records and .lst addresses
                          shift accordingly. Output bytes still start at offset
                          0 (no leading padding). Accepts 0x-prefixed hex,
                          h-suffixed hex, or decimal. Default: 0.

EXIT CODES:
    0    clean assembly
    1    assembly errors (one per line on stderr)
    2    usage / IO errors

When writing .lgo or .bin to stdout, the destination must not be a terminal.
Redirect to a file or use an explicit path.

                          h-suffixed hex, or decimal. Default: 0.

EXIT CODES:
    0    clean assembly
    1    assembly errors (one per line on stderr)
    2    usage / IO errors

When writing .lgo or .bin to stdout, the destination must not be a terminal.
Redirect to a file or use an explicit path.

; rpg2.s -- RPG-II Processor for COR24

; Prints first 10 bytes of each record to UART, then halts.

;
; Register allocation (frozen):
;   r0 = W (work/scratch)
;   r1 = RSP (return stack pointer, grows down from 0x0F0000)
;   r2 = IP (instruction pointer for threaded code)
;   sp = DSP (data stack, hardware push/pop in EBR)
;   fp = limited scratch (many instructions don't support fp)
;
; UART: data at 0xFF0100 (-65280), status at 0xFF0101 (-65279)
;   TX busy = status bit 7, RX ready = status bit 0

content.text:
**Yes — substantially unblocked.** Major state changes:

- `snobol4.lgo` (+ `snobol4.bin`) is now deployed at `$TOOLROOT/../lib/cor24/` (May 8 14:44 timestamps).
- `snobol4` wrapper is on PATH at `$TOOLROOT/snobol4`.
- dcsno shipped via the documented workaround for the dcemu `--lgo X.lgo --load-binary Y@addr` bug. The canonical `--uart-file` invocation pattern is *still* affected by that bug.

**For this saga (Path A): completely unblocked.** Just verified — `examples/hello.lgo` prints `Hello, World!\n` correctly when assembled from the hand-written `hello.s`. 619→408 instructions, exit 0. Path A doesn't touch SNOBOL4 at all so the dcemu bug is irrelevant here.

**For `scripts/verify-snobol4.sh`:** still failing — but for a *new* reason. It uses the broken `--lgo snobol4.lgo --uart-file test_builtins.sno` pattern; only 397 instructions execute, no OUTPUT. The fix is to switch to dcsno's documented workaround (`--load-binary snobol4.bin@0 --load-binary <prog>@0x080000 --entry 0`), but that's out of scope for the hello-world saga — I'll note it for a follow-up.

Continuing the hello-world saga. Step 001 is done (hello.s authored, hello.lgo built and verified). Committing it now and moving to step 002.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# fortran -- compile an FTI-0 .f source to COR24 assembly.
#
# Usage:
#   fortran <input.f>            # writes COR24 assembly to stdout
#   fortran --help
#
# CURRENT STATUS: Path A only.
#
# This wrapper is a Path-A fixture per
#   tools/briefs/dcftn-fortran-hello-world.md
# It short-circuits for the canonical Hello-World source
#   examples/hello.f
# by emitting the pre-baked
#   examples/hello.s
# on stdout. Any other input errors out -- the full FTI-0 compiler
# (the SNOBOL4-based pipeline that consumes arbitrary .f sources)
# is a separate parallel effort tracked under the milestone-1
# saga (currently archived behind the dcemu --lgo + --uart-file
# bug; see docs/snobol4-blockers.md).
#
# When the full compiler is ready, this wrapper will grow a Path B
# branch that invokes
#   cor24-emu --lgo $TOOLROOT/../lib/cor24/snobol4.lgo \
#             --uart-file <our compiler.sno> \
#             -u "$(cat <input.f>)"<EOT>
# instead.
#
# End-to-end pipeline (Path A):
#
#   scripts/fortran examples/hello.f > /tmp/hello.s
#   cor24-asm /tmp/hello.s -o /tmp/hello.lgo
#   cor24-emu --lgo /tmp/hello.lgo --quiet --speed 0 -n 1000000
#       => prints "Hello, World!\n", exit 0

set -euo pipefail

SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "$0")" && pwd)"
REPO_DIR="$(cd "$SCRIPT_DIR/.." && pwd)"

if [ "${1:-}" = "--help" ] || [ "${1:-}" = "-h" ]; then
    sed -n '2,/^$/p' "$0" | sed 's/^# \?//'
    exit 0
fi

if [ $# -lt 1 ]; then
    echo "fortran: error: expected one argument (an .f source file)" >&2
    echo "Usage: fortran <input.f>" >&2
    exit 2
fi

INPUT_F="$1"
if [ ! -f "$INPUT_F" ]; then
    echo "fortran: error: $INPUT_F not found." >&2
    exit 1
fi

INPUT_BASENAME="$(basename "$INPUT_F")"
HELLO_S="$REPO_DIR/examples/hello.s"

# Path A short-circuit: hello.f -> pre-baked hello.s.
if [ "$INPUT_BASENAME" = "hello.f" ]; then
    if [ ! -f "$HELLO_S" ]; then
        echo "fortran: error: $HELLO_S missing -- repo state inconsistent." >&2
        exit 1
    fi
    cat "$HELLO_S"
    exit 0
fi

# Path B is not implemented yet.
cat >&2 <<EOF
fortran: error: only hello.f is supported in the current Path-A
short-circuit. Input '$INPUT_BASENAME' would require the full FTI-0
compiler, which is a separate parallel effort.

  See: docs/snobol4-blockers.md
       tools/briefs/dcftn-fortran-hello-world.md
EOF
exit 1

toolUseResult.originalFile:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# fortran -- compile an FTI-0 .f source via the SNOBOL4-implemented compiler.
#
# Usage:
#   fortran <input.f>            # emits PL/SW (or assembly) to stdout
#   fortran --help
#
# Layered toolchain (build-time only; runtime is just cor24-emu):
#
#   .f source
#     |
#     |  cor24-emu --lgo <snobol4.lgo>
#     |    --uart-file <our compiler.sno>
#     |    -u "<.f source>"<EOT>
#     v
#   PL/SW or assembly text on UART output
#
# This wrapper assumes:
#   1. cor24-emu is on PATH (devgroup default).
#   2. snobol4.lgo is installed at $TOOLROOT/../lib/cor24/snobol4.lgo.
#      Not yet deployed at the time this wrapper landed -- see
#      docs/snobol4-blockers.md.
#   3. The FTI-0 compiler source is at <repo>/snobol4/src/driver.sno.
#      Currently a stub -- see the agentrail saga in .agentrail/.

set -euo pipefail

SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "$0")" && pwd)"
REPO_DIR="$(cd "$SCRIPT_DIR/.." && pwd)"

if [ "${1:-}" = "--help" ] || [ "${1:-}" = "-h" ]; then
    sed -n '2,/^$/p' "$0" | sed 's/^# \?//'
    exit 0
fi

if [ $# -lt 1 ]; then
    echo "fortran: error: expected one argument (an .f source file)" >&2
    echo "Usage: fortran <input.f>" >&2
    exit 2
fi

INPUT_F="$1"
if [ ! -f "$INPUT_F" ]; then
    echo "fortran: error: $INPUT_F not found." >&2
    exit 1
fi

# Locate snobol4.lgo via TOOLROOT (devgroup convention) or by deriving
# from pl-sw's install location (mirrors the pl-sw wrapper itself).
if [ -z "${TOOLROOT:-}" ]; then
    if PL_SW_PATH=$(command -v pl-sw 2>/dev/null); then
        TOOLROOT="$(dirname "$PL_SW_PATH")"
    fi
fi

if [ -z "${TOOLROOT:-}" ]; then
    echo "fortran: error: TOOLROOT not set and pl-sw is not on PATH." >&2
    echo "  Source the devgroup environment or export TOOLROOT to the dir" >&2
    echo "  containing pl-sw / cor24-emu." >&2
    exit 2
fi

LGO_DIR="$TOOLROOT/../lib/cor24"
SNOBOL4_LGO="$LGO_DIR/snobol4.lgo"
COMPILER_SNO="$REPO_DIR/snobol4/src/driver.sno"

if [ ! -f "$SNOBOL4_LGO" ]; then
    cat >&2 <<EOF
fortran: error: $SNOBOL4_LGO not found.

  The SNOBOL4 interpreter has not yet been built and installed at the
  system path. The dcsno saga in sw-embed/sw-cor24-snobol4 is
  responsible for producing snobol4.lgo and depositing it in
  ${LGO_DIR}/.

  See: docs/snobol4-blockers.md
EOF
    exit 1
fi

if [ ! -f "$COMPILER_SNO" ]; then
    echo "fortran: error: $COMPILER_SNO not found." >&2
    exit 1
fi

# Build UART input: the user's .f source, terminated with EOT (\x04).
USER_SRC="$(cat "$INPUT_F")"

exec cor24-emu \
    --lgo "$SNOBOL4_LGO" \
    --uart-file "$COMPILER_SNO" \
    -u "${USER_SRC}"$'\x04' \
    --speed 0 \
    -n 1000000000 \
    -t 600

input.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# test-hello.sh -- regression test for the Path A hello-world pipeline.
#
# Runs the full chain:
#   scripts/fortran examples/hello.f
#     | cor24-asm -> .lgo
#     | cor24-emu --lgo -> UART output
# and diffs the captured UART output against examples/hello.expected.
#
# Exit codes:
#   0 -- pass: UART output matches expected
#   1 -- fail: output differs (diff printed)
#   2 -- blocked: cor24-asm or cor24-emu missing on PATH

set -euo pipefail

SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "$0")" && pwd)"
REPO_DIR="$(cd "$SCRIPT_DIR/.." && pwd)"

for tool in cor24-asm cor24-emu; do
    if ! command -v "$tool" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
        echo "test-hello: BLOCKED -- '$tool' not on PATH." >&2
        exit 2
    fi
done

EXPECTED="$REPO_DIR/examples/hello.expected"
INPUT_F="$REPO_DIR/examples/hello.f"

if [ ! -f "$EXPECTED" ] || [ ! -f "$INPUT_F" ]; then
    echo "test-hello: error: missing fixture under $REPO_DIR/examples/" >&2
    exit 1
fi

WORK="$(mktemp -d)"
trap 'rm -rf "$WORK"' EXIT

"$REPO_DIR/scripts/fortran" "$INPUT_F" > "$WORK/hello.s"
cor24-asm "$WORK/hello.s" -o "$WORK/hello.lgo" >/dev/null
ACTUAL=$(cor24-emu --lgo "$WORK/hello.lgo" --quiet --speed 0 -n 1000000 2>/dev/null)

if printf '%s\n' "$ACTUAL" | diff -u "$EXPECTED" - >"$WORK/diff"; then
    echo "test-hello: OK -- pipeline produces 'Hello, World!'."
    exit 0
fi

echo "test-hello: FAIL -- output differs from expected:" >&2
cat "$WORK/diff" >&2
exit 1

content.content:
# sw-cor24-fortran

Fortran compiler for the COR24 24-bit RISC ISA (future/research).

Part of the [COR24 ecosystem](https://github.com/sw-embed/sw-cor24-project).

**FTI-0**  
*A small educational FORTRAN subset compiler in SNOBOL4 targeting PL/SW.*

## Overview

`sw-cor24-fortran` is an experimental educational compiler project.

The first language subset is **FTI-0**:

- a small, fixed-form, integer-only FORTRAN-like subset
- implemented in **SNOBOL4**
- targeting **PL/SW**
- designed for clarity, testability, and historical flavor rather than full FORTRAN compatibility

The initial goal is to compile small educational programs such as:

- integer assignment
- simple expressions
- `GOTO`
- counted `DO`
- `PRINT`
- `READ`
- one-dimensional integer arrays

The project is intentionally staged:

1. normalize and parse fixed-form source
2. build symbols and resolve labels
3. lower control flow to a simple normalized form
4. emit readable PL/SW
5. later revisit floating point and richer FORTRAN features

## Status

This repository currently defines:

- the project structure
- the FTI-0 language subset
- the compiler architecture
- the PL/SW mapping contract
- the milestone plan

Compiler implementation is expected to begin with source normalization and statement classification.

## Live demo: hello world

A working `Hello, World!` end-to-end demo is live, even though the
full FTI-0 compiler is still under construction:

```
scripts/fortran examples/hello.f > /tmp/hello.s
cor24-asm /tmp/hello.s -o /tmp/hello.lgo
cor24-emu --lgo /tmp/hello.lgo --quiet --speed 0
# prints "Hello, World!"
```

For now, `scripts/fortran` short-circuits `examples/hello.f` to a
hand-written `examples/hello.s` (Path A per
[`tools/briefs/dcftn-fortran-hello-world.md`](file:///disk1/github/softwarewrighter/devgroup/tools/briefs/dcftn-fortran-hello-world.md)).
The pre-built [`examples/hello.lgo`](examples/hello.lgo) is the demo
deliverable that the dwftn web frontend will embed.

Run `scripts/test-hello.sh` to regression-check the pipeline.

The full FTI-0 compiler (the SNOBOL4-based pipeline that consumes
arbitrary `.f` sources) is a separate parallel effort -- see
[`docs/snobol4-blockers.md`](docs/snobol4-blockers.md) for status.

## Status

This repository currently defines:

- the project structure
- the FTI-0 language subset
- the compiler architecture
- the PL/SW mapping contract
- the milestone plan
- a Path-A hello-world fixture (`examples/hello.s` + `examples/hello.lgo`)

Compiler implementation is expected to begin with source normalization and statement classification.

# sw-cor24-fortran

Fortran compiler for the COR24 24-bit RISC ISA (future/research).

Part of the [COR24 ecosystem](https://github.com/sw-embed/sw-cor24-project).

**FTI-0**  
*A small educational FORTRAN subset compiler in SNOBOL4 targeting PL/SW.*

## Overview

`sw-cor24-fortran` is an experimental educational compiler project.

The first language subset is **FTI-0**:

- a small, fixed-form, integer-only FORTRAN-like subset
- implemented in **SNOBOL4**
- targeting **PL/SW**
- designed for clarity, testability, and historical flavor rather than full FORTRAN compatibility

The initial goal is to compile small educational programs such as:

- integer assignment
- simple expressions
- `GOTO`
- counted `DO`
- `PRINT`
- `READ`
- one-dimensional integer arrays

The project is intentionally staged:

1. normalize and parse fixed-form source
2. build symbols and resolve labels
3. lower control flow to a simple normalized form
4. emit readable PL/SW
5. later revisit floating point and richer FORTRAN features

## Status

This repository currently defines:

- the project structure
- the FTI-0 language subset
- the compiler architecture
- the PL/SW mapping contract
- the milestone plan

Compiler implementation is expected to begin with source normalization and statement classification.

## Repository structure

- `docs/` — product, architecture, design, plan, subset definition
- `snobol4/` — compiler source and tests
- `examples/` — sample FTI-0 input programs
- `plsw/` — runtime helpers and generated output
- `scripts/` — development helpers

## First milestone

The first executable milestone is:

- accept a tiny `.f` source file
- normalize fixed-form statements
- classify statements
- dump normalized statement records

No code generation is required for the first milestone.

## Naming

- Repository: `sw-cor24-fortran`
- Subset language: `FTI-0`
- Compiler executable: `fti0c`

## Non-goals for FTI-0

FTI-0 does **not** initially support:

- floating point
- `FORMAT`
- `COMMON`
- `EQUIVALENCE`
- statement functions
- separate compilation
- optimization
- multidimensional arrays
- full FORTRAN compatibility

## Long-term direction

Possible later stages include:

- richer FORTRAN subset levels
- arithmetic IF
- subroutines and functions
- software floating point
- direct or indirect lowering into COR24-oriented toolchains

## Research

See `docs/research.txt` for detailed design research and historical context.

## Related Repositories

| Repository | Description |
|---|---|
| [sw-cor24-emulator](https://github.com/sw-embed/sw-cor24-emulator) | COR24 emulator + ISA |
| [sw-cor24-x-assembler](https://github.com/sw-embed/sw-cor24-x-assembler) | COR24 assembler |
| [sw-cor24-project](https://github.com/sw-embed/sw-cor24-project) | Ecosystem hub |

## Links

- Blog: [Software Wrighter Lab](https://software-wrighter-lab.github.io/)
- Discord: [Join the community](https://discord.com/invite/Ctzk5uHggZ)
- YouTube: [Software Wrighter](https://www.youtube.com/@SoftwareWrighter)

## Copyright

Copyright (c) 2026 Michael A. Wright

## License

MIT License. See [LICENSE](LICENSE) for the full text.

61	## Status (2026-05-08)
62	
63	The blocker has shifted. The original primitives issue (snobol4#1)
64	appears resolved at the source level. The live blocker is now
65	**toolchain deployment**: there is no `snobol4` wrapper on PATH and
66	no `snobol4.lgo` at `$TOOLROOT/../lib/cor24/`, so the SNOBOL4
67	interpreter FTI-0 sits on top of cannot be invoked.
68	
69	### Source level: issue #1 appears fixed
70	
71	In `sw-cor24-snobol4`:
72	
73	1. `docs/plan.md` line 499 (April 2026 status section):
74	   > "SIZE / SUBSTR / CHAR builtins added (issue #1) ..."
75	2. `src/sno_exec.plsw` line 1002+ contains a real `EXEC_BUILTIN`
76	   proc with substantive `WHEN (X_OP = OP_SIZE / OP_SUBSTR / OP_CHAR)`
77	   arms (not stubs).
78	
79	End-to-end verification is gated on deployment, below.
80	
81	### Deployment is owned by a coordinator brief
82	
83	The COR24 toolchain has consolidated onto a PATH-resolved model: each
84	language layer ships as `<lang>.lgo` at `$TOOLROOT/../lib/cor24/`
85	plus a one-line wrapper at `$TOOLROOT/<lang>` doing
86	`exec cor24-emu --lgo <lgo> "$@"`. PL/SW is live (`pl-sw`,
87	`plsw.lgo`); SNOBOL4 is not.
88	
89	The unblock is tracked at:
90	
91	- `/disk1/github/softwarewrighter/devgroup/tools/briefs/dcsno-bootstrap-snobol4-toolchain.md`
92	  (owner: dcsno; branch: `pr/bootstrap-toolchain`)
93	- Status per `tools/briefs/README.md` as of 2026-05-08: *"cleared,
94	  placeholder branch only"* -- dcsno can start; nothing shipped yet.
95	  The brief explicitly names dcftn as the downstream beneficiary
96	  (line 83 of that brief).
97	
98	When dcsno ships and mike relays, mike installs the artifacts and
99	dcftn is unblocked. No action in this repo will accelerate that.
100	
101	### What this repo has ready to verify
102	
103	Once `snobol4.lgo` is installed, three pieces wire up end-to-end
104	with no further changes here:
105	
106	- `scripts/verify-snobol4.sh` -- runs the test fixture through the
107	  interpreter, diffs against expected output, exit 0/1/2 for
108	  pass/fail/blocked.
109	- `snobol4/tests/builtins/` -- SIZE/SUBSTR/CHAR fixture mirroring
110	  the upstream `test_builtins.sno`.
111	- `scripts/fortran` -- user-facing `.f -> compiled output` wrapper.
112	  Errors with a pointer to this doc when `snobol4.lgo` is missing.
113	
114	## Next steps once `snobol4` is on PATH
115

appears resolved at the source level. The live blocker is now
**toolchain deployment**: there is no `snobol4` wrapper on PATH and
no `snobol4.lgo` at `$TOOLROOT/../lib/cor24/`, so the SNOBOL4
interpreter FTI-0 sits on top of cannot be invoked.

### Source level: issue #1 appears fixed

In `sw-cor24-snobol4`:

1. `docs/plan.md` line 499 (April 2026 status section):
   > "SIZE / SUBSTR / CHAR builtins added (issue #1) ..."
2. `src/sno_exec.plsw` line 1002+ contains a real `EXEC_BUILTIN`
   proc with substantive `WHEN (X_OP = OP_SIZE / OP_SUBSTR / OP_CHAR)`
   arms (not stubs).

End-to-end verification is gated on deployment, below.

### Deployment is owned by a coordinator brief

The COR24 toolchain has consolidated onto a PATH-resolved model: each
language layer ships as `<lang>.lgo` at `$TOOLROOT/../lib/cor24/`
plus a one-line wrapper at `$TOOLROOT/<lang>` doing
`exec cor24-emu --lgo <lgo> "$@"`. PL/SW is live (`pl-sw`,
`plsw.lgo`); SNOBOL4 is not.

The unblock is tracked at:

- `/disk1/github/softwarewrighter/devgroup/tools/briefs/dcsno-bootstrap-snobol4-toolchain.md`
  (owner: dcsno; branch: `pr/bootstrap-toolchain`)
- Status per `tools/briefs/README.md` as of 2026-05-08: *"cleared,
  placeholder branch only"* -- dcsno can start; nothing shipped yet.
  The brief explicitly names dcftn as the downstream beneficiary
  (line 83 of that brief).

When dcsno ships and mike relays, mike installs the artifacts and
dcftn is unblocked. No action in this repo will accelerate that.

### What this repo has ready to verify

Once `snobol4.lgo` is installed, three pieces wire up end-to-end
with no further changes here:

- `scripts/verify-snobol4.sh` -- runs the test fixture through the
  interpreter, diffs against expected output, exit 0/1/2 for
  pass/fail/blocked.
- `snobol4/tests/builtins/` -- SIZE/SUBSTR/CHAR fixture mirroring
  the upstream `test_builtins.sno`.
- `scripts/fortran` -- user-facing `.f -> compiled output` wrapper.
  Errors with a pointer to this doc when `snobol4.lgo` is missing.

## Next steps once `snobol4` is on PATH

## Status (2026-05-08, late)

**Update:** dcsno has shipped. `snobol4.lgo`, `snobol4.bin`, and the
`snobol4` wrapper are now installed at `$TOOLROOT/../lib/cor24/` and
`$TOOLROOT/`. The original primitives issue (snobol4#1) is resolved

1. **dcemu `--lgo` + `--uart-file` bug** -- the canonical invocation
   `cor24-emu --lgo snobol4.lgo --uart-file <prog>.sno` silently
   drops the input. `scripts/verify-snobol4.sh` exhibits this
   today: the SNOBOL4 interpreter loads (164 KB), reads the first
   `*` byte off UART, and halts after 397 instructions with no
   OUTPUT. Tracked at
   `tools/briefs/dcemu-lgo-load-binary-merge.md`. dcsno's
   `snobol4` wrapper uses the documented workaround
   (`cor24-emu --load-binary snobol4.bin@0 --load-binary
   <prog>@0x080000 --entry 0`) which sidesteps the bug.
2. **FTI-0 compiler not yet implemented.** Even with a working
   SNOBOL4 invocation, the milestone-1 saga is archived behind the
   blocker -- `snobol4/src/normalize.sno` is a draft, the
   classify/expr/symbols/labels/lower/emit_plsw phases are all
   stubs. Path B (full compile of `.f`) is many sessions of work.

What this means in practice (2026-05-08):

- The Hello World live demo is **unblocked via Path A** -- a
  hand-written `examples/hello.s` + pre-built `examples/hello.lgo`,
  with `scripts/fortran` short-circuiting `examples/hello.f` to
  the prebaked assembly. Verified end-to-end: `scripts/test-hello.sh`
  passes; `cor24-emu --lgo examples/hello.lgo --quiet` emits
  `Hello, World!`.

  due to the dcemu bug. Updating it to dcsno's binary-only
  workaround pattern is a follow-up; out of scope for the
  fortran-hello-world saga.
- The full FTI-0 compiler effort lives in the archived
  `milestone-1-source-normalization` saga
  (`.agentrail-archive/`), waiting for either the dcemu fix or a
  decision to use the workaround pattern from this repo too.
