toolUseResult.originalFile:
# SNOBOL4 toolchain blockers for FTI-0 milestone 1

## Summary

FTI-0's source normalizer (`snobol4/src/normalize.sno`) cannot be
implemented or executed without three SNOBOL4 builtin functions:
`SIZE`, `SUBSTR`, and `CHAR`. These were missing from the
`sw-embed/sw-cor24-snobol4` interpreter when milestone-1 work began.
The blocker was filed there as **issue #1**.

**Status as of 2026-05-08:** the *original* blocker (issue #1, missing
primitives) appears resolved upstream. A second *deployment* blocker is
now in front of us: the SNOBOL4 interpreter image (`snobol4.lgo`) has
not yet been built and installed at the system tool location
(`$TOOLROOT/../lib/cor24/snobol4.lgo`). Without that file, no
verification or downstream FTI-0 work can run end-to-end.

This session added the runtime plumbing in our repo (`scripts/fortran`,
`scripts/verify-snobol4.sh`, `snobol4/tests/builtins/`) so that the
moment `snobol4.lgo` lands, verification and compilation will Just Work
without further changes here.

## What FTI-0 needs from SNOBOL4

The source normalizer is a fixed-form column-aware text processor. Per
`docs/subset-fti0.md` and `docs/architecture.md`, it must:

1. read each physical input line
2. detect comments (`C` or `*` in column 1)
3. extract the optional numeric label from columns 1-5
4. detect a continuation marker in column 6 (non-blank and non-`0`)
5. extract the statement text from columns 7-72 (anything beyond col 72
   is ignored)
6. merge continuation lines into a single logical statement
7. emit normalized statement records preserving source line mapping

Implementing this requires column-precise substring extraction and
string length operations on every input line.

## The original blocker: SIZE / SUBSTR / CHAR

| Builtin | Signature | Why normalize.sno needs it |
|---------|-----------|----------------------------|
| `SIZE`   | `n = SIZE(s)`             | Measure line length: detect short lines (don't read past EOL) and blank lines. |
| `SUBSTR` | `t = SUBSTR(s, pos, len)` | Extract column ranges: cols 1-5 (label), col 6 (continuation marker), cols 7-72 (statement text). The whole normalizer hinges on this. |
| `CHAR`   | `c = CHAR(code)`          | Useful for character-class tests (e.g., is col 6 a non-blank non-`0`?) and for emitting specific characters in normalized output. |

Without these, no fixed-form column extraction is possible, and the
normalizer cannot be written.

## Upstream issue

`sw-embed/sw-cor24-snobol4#1`

This issue was filed by the agent that attempted milestone-1 step 002
(implement-normalize) and discovered the missing primitives. See
`.agentrail/steps/002-implement-normalize/summary.md` in this repo for
that agent's closure note ("Blocked: SNOBOL4 missing SIZE/SUBSTR/CHAR
-- issue sw-embed/sw-cor24-snobol4#1").

## Status (2026-05-08)

The blocker has shifted. The original primitives issue (snobol4#1)
appears resolved at the source level. The live blocker is now
**toolchain deployment**: there is no `snobol4` wrapper on PATH and
no `snobol4.lgo` at `$TOOLROOT/../lib/cor24/`, so the SNOBOL4
interpreter FTI-0 sits on top of cannot be invoked.

### Source level: issue #1 appears fixed

In `sw-cor24-snobol4`:

1. `docs/plan.md` line 499 (April 2026 status section):
   > "SIZE / SUBSTR / CHAR builtins added (issue #1) ..."
2. `src/sno_exec.plsw` line 1002+ contains a real `EXEC_BUILTIN`
   proc with substantive `WHEN (X_OP = OP_SIZE / OP_SUBSTR / OP_CHAR)`
   arms (not stubs).

End-to-end verification is gated on deployment, below.

### Deployment is owned by a coordinator brief

The COR24 toolchain has consolidated onto a PATH-resolved model: each
language layer ships as `<lang>.lgo` at `$TOOLROOT/../lib/cor24/`
plus a one-line wrapper at `$TOOLROOT/<lang>` doing
`exec cor24-emu --lgo <lgo> "$@"`. PL/SW is live (`pl-sw`,
`plsw.lgo`); SNOBOL4 is not.

The unblock is tracked at:

- `/disk1/github/softwarewrighter/devgroup/tools/briefs/dcsno-bootstrap-snobol4-toolchain.md`
  (owner: dcsno; branch: `pr/bootstrap-toolchain`)
- Status per `tools/briefs/README.md` as of 2026-05-08: *"cleared,
  placeholder branch only"* -- dcsno can start; nothing shipped yet.
  The brief explicitly names dcftn as the downstream beneficiary
  (line 83 of that brief).

When dcsno ships and mike relays, mike installs the artifacts and
dcftn is unblocked. No action in this repo will accelerate that.

### What this repo has ready to verify

Once `snobol4.lgo` is installed, three pieces wire up end-to-end
with no further changes here:

- `scripts/verify-snobol4.sh` -- runs the test fixture through the
  interpreter, diffs against expected output, exit 0/1/2 for
  pass/fail/blocked.
- `snobol4/tests/builtins/` -- SIZE/SUBSTR/CHAR fixture mirroring
  the upstream `test_builtins.sno`.
- `scripts/fortran` -- user-facing `.f -> compiled output` wrapper.
  Errors with a pointer to this doc when `snobol4.lgo` is missing.

## Next steps once `snobol4` is on PATH

When mike signals that the dcsno brief has shipped:

1. Sanity-check the deployment from this repo:
   ```
   scripts/verify-snobol4.sh
   ```
   Expect exit 0 and a one-line "OK" message. Exit 1 (FAIL) means the
   deployed interpreter doesn't match what FTI-0 assumes -- file a
   blocker on dcsno before touching anything else here.
2. In `.agentrail/`, the saga is parked at step 005-normalize-tests
   (formerly 004; renumbered when the verify-and-vendor step landed
   in front of it). Step 002 (implement-normalize) is marked
   completed but its work was never written -- `snobol4/src/normalize.sno`
   is still a stub.
   - `agentrail reopen 2` and re-attempt the implementation using
     SIZE / SUBSTR / CHAR.
   - When green, `agentrail reopen 5` (or 4, depending on numbering at
     that time) and run the test runner against the fixtures already
     authored in `snobol4/tests/normalize/`.
   - Then proceed to integrate-driver and the remaining FTI-0
     milestones (classify, expr, symbols/labels, lower, emit_plsw).
3. The `.f -> .s` end-to-end smoke test, once the compiler exists, is
   `scripts/fortran examples/hello.f`.

## Adjacent risks (might surface as new blockers)

The current blocker is just SIZE / SUBSTR / CHAR for the *normalizer*.
Later FTI-0 milestones will need more of the SNOBOL4 surface:

- **Pattern matching** (SPAN, BREAK, REM) -- needed for tokenization
  in the classify and expr phases. The sibling repo's plan.md and
  examples suggest these are implemented; worth reconfirming when
  classify lands.
- **TABLE / array-like data structures** -- needed for symbol and
  label tables (milestone 4). Sibling plan.md mentions `ARR_POOL`
  and array support; FTI-0 specifics need confirmation.
- **OUTPUT to a file vs. stdout** -- needed for emitting `.msw`
  files (milestone 6). Sibling plan.md notes UART TX as the OUTPUT
  sink; may require host-side tooling to capture into a file.
- **Integer width / overflow semantics** -- COR24 is 24-bit; need
  to confirm SNOBOL4 arithmetic integers fit and that overflow
  behavior is documented.

These are not currently blockers -- they are flagged so the next
blocker discovery isn't a surprise.

## Where this is tracked

Upstream (the actual unblock):

- `/disk1/github/softwarewrighter/devgroup/tools/briefs/dcsno-bootstrap-snobol4-toolchain.md`
  -- the saga brief mike will hand to dcsno.
- `/disk1/github/softwarewrighter/devgroup/tools/briefs/README.md`
  -- the rolling status table; check here before assuming the blocker
  is still live.

In this repo:

- `.agentrail/steps/002-implement-normalize/summary.md` -- original
  blocker note from the agent who first hit this
- `.agentrail/steps/005-normalize-tests/summary.md` (or `004-...` if
  renumbered) -- runner+verify step, blocked
- `snobol4/tests/builtins/README.md` -- the SIZE/SUBSTR/CHAR
  verification fixture authored this session
- `snobol4/tests/normalize/README.md` -- the normalize-phase fixtures
  authored in a prior session
- `scripts/fortran` and `scripts/verify-snobol4.sh` -- the runtime
  plumbing waiting on `snobol4.lgo`

When the deployment lands and FTI-0 actually runs end-to-end, update
or replace this document with a "lessons learned" / postmortem note,
or fold it into a milestone retrospective.

attachment.snippet:
1	# sw-cor24-fortran
2	
3	Fortran compiler for the COR24 24-bit RISC ISA (future/research).
4	
5	Part of the [COR24 ecosystem](https://github.com/sw-embed/sw-cor24-project).
6	
7	**FTI-0**  
8	*A small educational FORTRAN subset compiler in SNOBOL4 targeting PL/SW.*
9	
10	## Overview
11	
12	`sw-cor24-fortran` is an experimental educational compiler project.
13	
14	The first language subset is **FTI-0**:
15	
16	- a small, fixed-form, integer-only FORTRAN-like subset
17	- implemented in **SNOBOL4**
18	- targeting **PL/SW**
19	- designed for clarity, testability, and historical flavor rather than full FORTRAN compatibility
20	
21	The initial goal is to compile small educational programs such as:
22	
23	- integer assignment
24	- simple expressions
25	- `GOTO`
26	- counted `DO`
27	- `PRINT`
28	- `READ`
29	- one-dimensional integer arrays
30	
31	The project is intentionally staged:
32	
33	1. normalize and parse fixed-form source
34	2. build symbols and resolve labels
35	3. lower control flow to a simple normalized form
36	4. emit readable PL/SW
37	5. later revisit floating point and richer FORTRAN features
38	
39	## Live demo: hello world
40	
41	A working `Hello, World!` end-to-end demo is live, even though the
42	full FTI-0 compiler is still under construction:
43	
44	```
45	scripts/fortran examples/hello.f > /tmp/hello.s
46	cor24-asm /tmp/hello.s -o /tmp/hello.lgo
47	cor24-emu --lgo /tmp/hello.lgo --quiet --speed 0
48	# prints "Hello, World!"
49	```
50	
51	For now, `scripts/fortran` short-circuits `examples/hello.f` to a
52	hand-written `examples/hello.s` (Path A per
53	[`tools/briefs/dcftn-fortran-hello-world.md`](file:///disk1/github/softwarewrighter/devgroup/tools/briefs/dcftn-fortran-hello-world.md)).
54	The pre-built [`examples/hello.lgo`](examples/hello.lgo) is the demo
55	deliverable that the dwftn web frontend will embed.
56	
57	Run `scripts/test-hello.sh` to regression-check the pipeline.
58	
59	The full FTI-0 compiler (the SNOBOL4-based pipeline that consumes
60	arbitrary `.f` sources) is a separate parallel effort -- see
61	[`docs/snobol4-blockers.md`](docs/snobol4-blockers.md) for status.
62	
63	## Status
64	
65	This repository currently defines:
66	
67	- the project structure
68	- the FTI-0 language subset
69	- the compiler architecture
70	- the PL/SW mapping contract
71	- the milestone plan
72	- a Path-A hello-world fixture (`examples/hello.s` + `examples/hello.lgo`)
73	
74	Compiler implementation is expected to begin with source normalization and statement classification.
75	
76	## Repository structure
77	
78	- `docs/` -- product, architecture, design, plan, subset definition
79	- `snobol4/` -- compiler source and tests
80	- `examples/` -- sample FTI-0 input programs
81	- `plsw/` -- runtime helpers and generated output
82	- `scripts/` -- development helpers
83	
84	## First milestone
85	
86	The first executable milestone is:
87	
88	- accept a tiny `.f` source file
89	- normalize fixed-form statements
90	- classify statements
91	- dump normalized statement records
92	
93	No code generation is required for the first milestone.
94	
95	## Naming
96	
97	- Repository: `sw-cor24-fortran`
98	- Subset language: `FTI-0`
99	- Compiler executable: `fti0c`
100	
101	## Non-goals for FTI-0
102	
103	FTI-0 does **not** initially support:
104	
105	- floating point
106	- `FORMAT`
107	- `COMMON`
108	- `EQUIVALENCE`
109	- statement functions
110	- separate compilation
111	- optimization
112	- multidimensional arrays
113	- full FORTRAN compatibility
114	
115	## Long-term direction
116	
117	Possible later stages include:
118	
119	- richer FORTRAN subset levels
120	- arithmetic IF
121	- subroutines and functions
122	- software floating point
123	- direct or indirect lowering into COR24-oriented toolchains
124	
125	## Research
126	
127	See `docs/research.txt` for detailed design research and historical context.
128	
129	## Related Repositories
130	
131	| Repository | Description |
132	|---|---|
133	| [sw-cor24-emulator](https://github.com/sw-embed/sw-cor24-emulator) | COR24 emulator + ISA |
134	| [sw-cor24-x-assembler](https://github.com/sw-embed/sw-cor24-x-assembler) | COR24 assembler |
135	| [sw-cor24-project](https://github.com/sw-embed/sw-cor24-project) | Ecosystem hub |
136

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# fortran -- compile an FTI-0 .f source to COR24 assembly.
#
# Usage:
#   fortran <input.f>            # writes COR24 assembly to stdout
#   fortran --help
#
# CURRENT STATUS: Path A only.
#
# This wrapper is a Path-A fixture per
#   tools/briefs/dcftn-fortran-hello-world.md
# It short-circuits for the canonical Hello-World source
#   examples/hello.f
# by emitting the pre-baked
#   examples/hello.s
# on stdout. Any other input errors out -- the full FTI-0 compiler
# (the SNOBOL4-based pipeline that consumes arbitrary .f sources)
# is a separate parallel effort tracked under the milestone-1
# saga (currently archived behind the dcemu --lgo + --uart-file
# bug; see docs/snobol4-blockers.md).
#
# When the full compiler is ready, this wrapper will grow a Path B
# branch that invokes
#   cor24-emu --lgo $TOOLROOT/../lib/cor24/snobol4.lgo \
#             --uart-file <our compiler.sno> \
#             -u "$(cat <input.f>)"<EOT>
# instead.
#
# End-to-end pipeline (Path A):
#
#   scripts/fortran examples/hello.f > /tmp/hello.s
#   cor24-asm /tmp/hello.s -o /tmp/hello.lgo
#   cor24-emu --lgo /tmp/hello.lgo --quiet --speed 0 -n 1000000
#       => prints "Hello, World!\n", exit 0

set -euo pipefail

SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "$0")" && pwd)"
REPO_DIR="$(cd "$SCRIPT_DIR/.." && pwd)"

if [ "${1:-}" = "--help" ] || [ "${1:-}" = "-h" ]; then
    sed -n '2,/^$/p' "$0" | sed 's/^# \?//'
    exit 0
fi

if [ $# -lt 1 ]; then
    echo "fortran: error: expected one argument (an .f source file)" >&2
    echo "Usage: fortran <input.f>" >&2
    exit 2
fi

INPUT_F="$1"
if [ ! -f "$INPUT_F" ]; then
    echo "fortran: error: $INPUT_F not found." >&2
    exit 1
fi

INPUT_BASENAME="$(basename "$INPUT_F")"
HELLO_S="$REPO_DIR/examples/hello.s"

# Path A short-circuit: hello.f -> pre-baked hello.s.
if [ "$INPUT_BASENAME" = "hello.f" ]; then
    if [ ! -f "$HELLO_S" ]; then
        echo "fortran: error: $HELLO_S missing -- repo state inconsistent." >&2
        exit 1
    fi
    cat "$HELLO_S"
    exit 0
fi

# Path B is not implemented yet.
cat >&2 <<EOF
fortran: error: only hello.f is supported in the current Path-A
short-circuit. Input '$INPUT_BASENAME' would require the full FTI-0
compiler, which is a separate parallel effort.

  See: docs/snobol4-blockers.md
       tools/briefs/dcftn-fortran-hello-world.md
EOF
exit 1

=== installed fortran with proper exit code ===
exit=1
--stdout--
--stderr--
fortran: error: /disk1/github/softwarewrighter/devgroup/work/examples/hello.s missing -- repo state inconsistent.

content.text:
Two issues with the install state to flag — neither blocks dwftn, but worth knowing:

**1. `hello-fortran.lgo` isn't where you said.** It's NOT at `work/lib/cor24/hello-fortran.lgo`. It's at:
- `dcftn/.../sw-cor24-fortran/examples/hello.lgo` (my repo, source)
- `dwftn/.../web-sw-cor24-fortran/examples/hello.lgo` (mike copied directly to dwftn)

So dwftn IS unblocked (they have the file in their repo), but `lib/cor24/` only has `plsw.lgo`, `snobol4.lgo`, `snobol4.bin`. mike apparently picked a different deployment strategy than the brief described.

**2. The installed `fortran` wrapper is broken.** mike copied `scripts/fortran` verbatim to `$TOOLROOT/fortran`. The wrapper resolves `HELLO_S=$SCRIPT_DIR/../examples/hello.s` — when installed at `$TOOLROOT/`, that resolves to `/disk1/.../work/examples/hello.s` which doesn't exist:

```
$ fortran $(pwd)/examples/hello.f
fortran: error: /disk1/.../work/examples/hello.s missing -- repo state inconsistent.
exit=1
```

It works fine when run as `./scripts/fortran` from the repo. Just broken via PATH.

**The bug is mine.** I designed the wrapper for in-repo invocation only and didn't make it work from a system-install location. dwftn's saga doesn't depend on the PATH wrapper (they embed `hello.lgo` directly via `include_bytes!`), so it's not blocking the demo. But anyone trying `fortran hello.f` from a shell will hit it.

Two reasonable fixes:
1. **Embed `hello.s` as a heredoc inside the wrapper** — self-contained, no install coordination. ~115 lines of inline assembly, but it's the simplest option.
2. **Have mike install `hello.s` alongside `hello.lgo` at `$TOOLROOT/../lib/cor24/hello.s`** and update the wrapper to look there first (mirror of how `pl-sw` resolves `plsw.lgo`). Cleaner architecturally; needs install coordination.

Want me to ship a fix as `pr/fortran-wrapper-fix`? Recommend option 1 (heredoc) — fewer moving parts, no further mike-side work.

1	# Brief: ship a working `hello.f → hello.lgo → "Hello, World!"` demo
2	
3	**Owner:** dcftn
4	**Branch:** start as `feat/fortran-hello-world`; `dg-mark-pr` when ready (becomes `pr/fortran-hello-world`)
5	**Repo:** `sw-cor24-fortran`
6	**Prerequisite:** dcsno's `pr/bootstrap-toolchain` saga must be relayed and `snobol4.lgo` + `snobol4` wrapper installed on PATH. Mike will signal when ready.
7	
8	## What sw-cor24-fortran is and isn't
9	
10	`sw-cor24-fortran` is a **Fortran compiler** for COR24 (FTI-0 subset). The compiler is written in SNOBOL4 (a `fortran-compiler.sno` source file, eventually). It runs as a SNOBOL4 program on top of `snobol4.lgo`. **The compiler is the artifact** the repo produces — `hello.f` and other examples are *demos that exercise the compiler*, not the compiler itself.
11	
12	Two equally valid packaging models for the shipped compiler (mike picks at install time, agent doesn't have to choose):
13	
14	- **Script form:** `fortran` is a shell wrapper that composes at runtime: `cor24-emu --lgo $TOOLROOT/../lib/cor24/snobol4.lgo --load-binary $TOOLROOT/../lib/cor24/fortran-compiler.sno@0x080000 --entry 0 -u "$(cat <input.f>)"$'\x04' …`. Two installed files: `snobol4.lgo` (already shipped) + `fortran-compiler.sno` (you ship).
15	- **Bundled form:** at build time, mike combines `snobol4.lgo` + `fortran-compiler.sno` into a single `fortran-compiler.lgo`. Wrapper points at it directly. One installed file.
16	
17	Both are legitimate. Past me wrongly claimed the bundled form "would never exist by design"; that was wrong, and dcftn shouldn't be constrained by it. Either packaging works for any future Fortran-compiler saga — pick whichever feels cleaner when you get there.
18	
19	## This particular saga is for a *demo*, not the compiler itself
20	
21	Despite the long preamble: **this saga's scope is narrow** — it's specifically the **Hello-World demo**, not "build the Fortran compiler." Path A (hand-write hello.s, ship as a fixture) is the recommended approach. The full Fortran compiler is a much larger parallel effort, tracked separately under the FTI-0 milestone-1 saga in your repo.
22	
23	## Why this matters (revised framing)
24	
25	This is the **second of three sagas** unblocking the Fortran hello-world live demo at `https://sw-embed.github.io/web-sw-cor24-fortran/`. dcsno ships SNOBOL4 to PATH; you ship `hello.f` + a way to produce `hello.lgo` (Path A: hand-write); dwftn embeds the demo output in their web frontend. The Fortran-compiler-itself saga continues separately and *will* eventually exist as either `fortran-compiler.sno` (script form) or `fortran-compiler.lgo` (bundled form) at `work/lib/cor24/`. This saga is just the demo precursor.
26	
27	## Known dcemu bug affecting the SNOBOL4 invocation pattern
28	
29	There's a verified bug (`tools/briefs/dcemu-lgo-load-binary-merge.md`) where `cor24-emu --lgo X.lgo --load-binary Y@addr` silently drops the `--load-binary`. dcsno's `snobol4` wrapper is using a documented workaround (binary-only invocation: `cor24-emu --load-binary snobol4.bin@0 --load-binary <input>@0x080000 --entry 0`) until dcemu fixes it.
30	
31	For your saga: this means **Path A (hand-write `hello.s`) is meaningfully simpler than Path B (run full SNOBOL4 compiler)** — Path A doesn't touch the SNOBOL4 wrapper at all and so isn't affected by the bug. **Pick Path A unless Path B is clearly faster.**
32	
33	## Goal
34	
35	When this saga ships, running:
36	
37	```
38	fortran examples/hello.f > /tmp/hello.s
39	cor24-asm /tmp/hello.s -o /tmp/hello.lgo
40	cor24-emu --lgo /tmp/hello.lgo --terminal --speed 0
41	```
42	
43	emits `Hello, World!` (followed by newline) on stdout/UART. The `examples/hello.f` is the canonical FORTRAN-IV-style hello world:
44	
45	```fortran
46	      PROGRAM HELLO
47	      PRINT *, 'Hello, World!'
48	      END
49	```
50	
51	(Or whatever flavor your FTI-0 subset supports; ASCII output is what matters.)
52	
53	## Acceptable scope reductions
54	
55	You're encouraged to **shortcut the full compiler** for this saga. Two flavors:
56	
57	### Path A: hand-write hello.s, ship as a fixture
58	
59	If the SNOBOL4-based compiler isn't ready to compile hello.f end-to-end:
60	
61	1. Write `examples/hello.f` with the canonical source.
62	2. Hand-write the equivalent COR24 assembly in `examples/hello.s` (or generate it via a one-shot run of your draft `normalize.sno` + downstream phases that DO work).
63	3. Update `scripts/fortran` so it short-circuits for hello.f: detect this specific input and emit the pre-baked `.s`, OR document that `scripts/fortran` is a stub for now and `examples/hello.s` is the canonical reference output until the compiler is complete.
64	4. Ship the assembly as a regression fixture (`work/reg-rs/hello.s.expected` or similar).
65	
66	This unblocks dwftn immediately. You then catch up the actual compiler in subsequent sagas.
67	
68	### Path B: actual end-to-end compilation
69	
70	If the compiler is far enough along (normalize.sno verified, parser/codegen drafted), wire the full pipeline and emit the assembly automatically. Then `scripts/fortran examples/hello.f` produces real output. Keep PRINT-statement-only scope — no expressions, no control flow, no I/O variants.
71	
72	**Pick Path A if it's any faster.** The goal is shipping the demo, not finishing the compiler.
73	
74	## Verification
75	
76	```bash
77	# Step 1: produce assembly
78	scripts/fortran examples/hello.f > /tmp/hello.s
79	
80	# Step 2: assemble
81	cor24-asm /tmp/hello.s -o /tmp/hello.lgo
82	
83	# Step 3: run; should print "Hello, World!"
84	cor24-emu --lgo /tmp/hello.lgo --terminal --speed 0
85	```
86	
87	Exit code 0, expected output captured as a regression fixture committed to the repo.
88	
89	Also run `scripts/verify-snobol4.sh` — if it returns 0 (SIZE/SUBSTR/CHAR all good), continue toward Path B. If it returns 2 (snobol4.lgo not deployed), block on dcsno. If 1 (builtins broken), file a precise saga back to dcsno.
90	
91	## What goes in this PR
92	
93	1. `examples/hello.f` — canonical hello world source.
94	2. `examples/hello.s` — corresponding COR24 assembly (hand-written or generated).
95	3. `examples/hello.lgo` — pre-built lgo (committed; dwftn embeds this directly until the live compile path works).
96	4. Updated `scripts/fortran` to handle hello.f end-to-end (Path A: short-circuit; Path B: full pipeline).
97	5. A regression test under `tests/` or `work/reg-rs/` that runs the pipeline and diffs against expected output.
98	6. README update: "Fortran hello world live demo: see `examples/hello.f` + `examples/hello.lgo`."
99	
100	## What does NOT go in this PR
101	
102	- No full FTI-0 compiler. Just hello world.
103	- No control flow (IF, DO, GOTO).
104	- No expressions beyond a string literal.
105	- No additional examples beyond hello.
106	- No web frontend work (that's dwftn).
107	
108	## When done
109	
110	Workflow: `dg-new-feature fortran-hello-world` → implement → `dg-mark-pr`. Signal mike. After mike relays:
111	- mike installs `examples/hello.lgo` to `work/lib/cor24/hello-fortran.lgo` (or just leaves it in your repo for dwftn to fetch directly)
112	- mike installs `scripts/fortran` to `work/bin/fortran` (the wrapper, similar to `pl-sw`/`snobol4`)
113	- mike clears dwftn to start their live-demo saga
114	
115	Promotion to `main` is mike's call, separately.
116	
117	## After this saga
118	
119	Subsequent sagas continue building out the FTI-0 compiler (more statements, expressions, control flow) without blocking the demo. The demo stays live throughout — it just renders a fixed hello.lgo. As your compiler grows, dwftn can eventually swap in dynamic compilation, but that's out of scope here.

file.content:
# Brief: ship a working `hello.f → hello.lgo → "Hello, World!"` demo

**Owner:** dcftn
**Branch:** start as `feat/fortran-hello-world`; `dg-mark-pr` when ready (becomes `pr/fortran-hello-world`)
**Repo:** `sw-cor24-fortran`
**Prerequisite:** dcsno's `pr/bootstrap-toolchain` saga must be relayed and `snobol4.lgo` + `snobol4` wrapper installed on PATH. Mike will signal when ready.

## What sw-cor24-fortran is and isn't

`sw-cor24-fortran` is a **Fortran compiler** for COR24 (FTI-0 subset). The compiler is written in SNOBOL4 (a `fortran-compiler.sno` source file, eventually). It runs as a SNOBOL4 program on top of `snobol4.lgo`. **The compiler is the artifact** the repo produces — `hello.f` and other examples are *demos that exercise the compiler*, not the compiler itself.

Two equally valid packaging models for the shipped compiler (mike picks at install time, agent doesn't have to choose):

- **Script form:** `fortran` is a shell wrapper that composes at runtime: `cor24-emu --lgo $TOOLROOT/../lib/cor24/snobol4.lgo --load-binary $TOOLROOT/../lib/cor24/fortran-compiler.sno@0x080000 --entry 0 -u "$(cat <input.f>)"$'\x04' …`. Two installed files: `snobol4.lgo` (already shipped) + `fortran-compiler.sno` (you ship).
- **Bundled form:** at build time, mike combines `snobol4.lgo` + `fortran-compiler.sno` into a single `fortran-compiler.lgo`. Wrapper points at it directly. One installed file.

Both are legitimate. Past me wrongly claimed the bundled form "would never exist by design"; that was wrong, and dcftn shouldn't be constrained by it. Either packaging works for any future Fortran-compiler saga — pick whichever feels cleaner when you get there.

## This particular saga is for a *demo*, not the compiler itself

Despite the long preamble: **this saga's scope is narrow** — it's specifically the **Hello-World demo**, not "build the Fortran compiler." Path A (hand-write hello.s, ship as a fixture) is the recommended approach. The full Fortran compiler is a much larger parallel effort, tracked separately under the FTI-0 milestone-1 saga in your repo.

## Why this matters (revised framing)

This is the **second of three sagas** unblocking the Fortran hello-world live demo at `https://sw-embed.github.io/web-sw-cor24-fortran/`. dcsno ships SNOBOL4 to PATH; you ship `hello.f` + a way to produce `hello.lgo` (Path A: hand-write); dwftn embeds the demo output in their web frontend. The Fortran-compiler-itself saga continues separately and *will* eventually exist as either `fortran-compiler.sno` (script form) or `fortran-compiler.lgo` (bundled form) at `work/lib/cor24/`. This saga is just the demo precursor.

## Known dcemu bug affecting the SNOBOL4 invocation pattern

There's a verified bug (`tools/briefs/dcemu-lgo-load-binary-merge.md`) where `cor24-emu --lgo X.lgo --load-binary Y@addr` silently drops the `--load-binary`. dcsno's `snobol4` wrapper is using a documented workaround (binary-only invocation: `cor24-emu --load-binary snobol4.bin@0 --load-binary <input>@0x080000 --entry 0`) until dcemu fixes it.

For your saga: this means **Path A (hand-write `hello.s`) is meaningfully simpler than Path B (run full SNOBOL4 compiler)** — Path A doesn't touch the SNOBOL4 wrapper at all and so isn't affected by the bug. **Pick Path A unless Path B is clearly faster.**

## Goal

When this saga ships, running:

```
fortran examples/hello.f > /tmp/hello.s
cor24-asm /tmp/hello.s -o /tmp/hello.lgo
cor24-emu --lgo /tmp/hello.lgo --terminal --speed 0
```

emits `Hello, World!` (followed by newline) on stdout/UART. The `examples/hello.f` is the canonical FORTRAN-IV-style hello world:

```fortran
      PROGRAM HELLO
      PRINT *, 'Hello, World!'
      END
```

(Or whatever flavor your FTI-0 subset supports; ASCII output is what matters.)

## Acceptable scope reductions

You're encouraged to **shortcut the full compiler** for this saga. Two flavors:

### Path A: hand-write hello.s, ship as a fixture

If the SNOBOL4-based compiler isn't ready to compile hello.f end-to-end:

1. Write `examples/hello.f` with the canonical source.
2. Hand-write the equivalent COR24 assembly in `examples/hello.s` (or generate it via a one-shot run of your draft `normalize.sno` + downstream phases that DO work).
3. Update `scripts/fortran` so it short-circuits for hello.f: detect this specific input and emit the pre-baked `.s`, OR document that `scripts/fortran` is a stub for now and `examples/hello.s` is the canonical reference output until the compiler is complete.
4. Ship the assembly as a regression fixture (`work/reg-rs/hello.s.expected` or similar).

This unblocks dwftn immediately. You then catch up the actual compiler in subsequent sagas.

### Path B: actual end-to-end compilation

If the compiler is far enough along (normalize.sno verified, parser/codegen drafted), wire the full pipeline and emit the assembly automatically. Then `scripts/fortran examples/hello.f` produces real output. Keep PRINT-statement-only scope — no expressions, no control flow, no I/O variants.

**Pick Path A if it's any faster.** The goal is shipping the demo, not finishing the compiler.

## Verification

```bash
# Step 1: produce assembly
scripts/fortran examples/hello.f > /tmp/hello.s

# Step 2: assemble
cor24-asm /tmp/hello.s -o /tmp/hello.lgo

# Step 3: run; should print "Hello, World!"
cor24-emu --lgo /tmp/hello.lgo --terminal --speed 0
```

Exit code 0, expected output captured as a regression fixture committed to the repo.

Also run `scripts/verify-snobol4.sh` — if it returns 0 (SIZE/SUBSTR/CHAR all good), continue toward Path B. If it returns 2 (snobol4.lgo not deployed), block on dcsno. If 1 (builtins broken), file a precise saga back to dcsno.

## What goes in this PR

1. `examples/hello.f` — canonical hello world source.
2. `examples/hello.s` — corresponding COR24 assembly (hand-written or generated).
3. `examples/hello.lgo` — pre-built lgo (committed; dwftn embeds this directly until the live compile path works).
4. Updated `scripts/fortran` to handle hello.f end-to-end (Path A: short-circuit; Path B: full pipeline).
5. A regression test under `tests/` or `work/reg-rs/` that runs the pipeline and diffs against expected output.
6. README update: "Fortran hello world live demo: see `examples/hello.f` + `examples/hello.lgo`."

## What does NOT go in this PR

- No full FTI-0 compiler. Just hello world.
- No control flow (IF, DO, GOTO).
- No expressions beyond a string literal.
- No additional examples beyond hello.
- No web frontend work (that's dwftn).

## When done

Workflow: `dg-new-feature fortran-hello-world` → implement → `dg-mark-pr`. Signal mike. After mike relays:
- mike installs `examples/hello.lgo` to `work/lib/cor24/hello-fortran.lgo` (or just leaves it in your repo for dwftn to fetch directly)
- mike installs `scripts/fortran` to `work/bin/fortran` (the wrapper, similar to `pl-sw`/`snobol4`)
- mike clears dwftn to start their live-demo saga

Promotion to `main` is mike's call, separately.

## After this saga

Subsequent sagas continue building out the FTI-0 compiler (more statements, expressions, control flow) without blocking the demo. The demo stays live throughout — it just renders a fixed hello.lgo. As your compiler grows, dwftn can eventually swap in dynamic compilation, but that's out of scope here.

content.content:
# Brief: stand up the `web-sw-cor24-fortran` live demo with hello-world

**Owner:** dwftn
**Branch:** start as `feat/hello-world-demo`; `dg-mark-pr` when ready (becomes `pr/hello-world-demo`)
**Repo:** `web-sw-cor24-fortran` (mike just created the bare; clone it as your primary)
**Prerequisite:** dcftn's `pr/fortran-hello-world` saga must be relayed and `examples/hello.lgo` must exist (either inside dcftn's repo or installed at `work/lib/cor24/hello-fortran.lgo`). Mike will signal when ready.

## Why this matters

This is the **third and final saga** unblocking the Fortran hello-world live demo at `https://sw-embed.github.io/web-sw-cor24-fortran/`. dcsno shipped SNOBOL4; dcftn produced `hello.lgo`; you embed it into a Yew/WASM web frontend that runs `cor24-emu` in the browser and shows the output. **Goal: someone visiting the URL sees "Hello, World!" rendered live** (not as static text — actually executed by the embedded emulator).

## One-time setup

Clone your repo from the bare:

```bash
mkdir -p ~/github/sw-embed && cd ~/github/sw-embed
git clone /disk1/github/softwarewrighter/devgroup/work/bare/web-sw-cor24-fortran.git
```

You also need the same Cargo path-deps every other web-sw-cor24-* repo uses:

```bash
[ -d sw-cor24-emulator ] || git clone /disk1/github/softwarewrighter/devgroup/work/bare/sw-cor24-emulator.git
[ -d sw-cor24-isa ]      || git clone /disk1/github/softwarewrighter/devgroup/work/bare/sw-cor24-isa.git
```

The repo is essentially empty (default README from `gh repo create`). You're starting fresh.

## Reference templates

The cleanest existing template is **`web-sw-cor24-x-tinyc`** (recently rebuilt, Trunk-based, embeds `cor24-emu` via the emulator's `cdylib`/wasm32 build). dwxtc just shipped a `pr/rebuild-pages-and-title` saga there — read their repo to mirror the pattern:

- `Cargo.toml` workspace shape (cor24-emulator + cor24-isa as path-deps)
- `Trunk.toml` for the WASM bundling
- `index.html` with the Yew root
- `src/main.rs` with the Yew app
- `scripts/build-pages.sh` that does `trunk build --release --public-url /web-sw-cor24-fortran/` and rsyncs `dist/` to `pages/`
- `.github/workflows/pages.yml` that uploads `pages/` and deploys

- `scripts/build-pages.sh` that does `trunk build --release --public-url /web-sw-cor24-fortran/` and rsyncs `dist/` to `pages/`
- `.github/workflows/pages.yml` that uploads `pages/` and deploys

lastPrompt: look for updated briefs or README

content.text:
Three updates today, none requiring my action:

**`dcftn-fortran-hello-world.md` (15:17)** — added a "What sw-cor24-fortran is and isn't" section that clarifies the project's nature: **the Fortran compiler is the artifact** (eventually `fortran-compiler.sno` or `fortran-compiler.lgo` at `lib/cor24/`); `hello.f` and friends are demos that exercise the compiler. Two equally-valid future packaging models documented (script form vs bundled form). Doesn't change anything I just shipped; just re-frames future Fortran-compiler-itself sagas. Past "bundled form by design impossible" claim retracted.

**`README.md` (15:17)** — my saga still shows as `🟡 gated on dcsno-bootstrap-snobol4-toolchain` (i.e., not yet flipped to ✅), even though `pr/fortran-hello-world` is merged into dev and reaped. Index lag, not a content change. Worth poking mike if other agents are reading status off the README.

**`dwftn-hello-world-demo.md` (15:18)** — their prereq line says: *"dcftn's `pr/fortran-hello-world` saga must be relayed and `examples/hello.lgo` must exist (either inside dcftn's repo or installed at `work/lib/cor24/hello-fortran.lgo`)."* Both conditions are satisfied (file in my repo at `examples/hello.lgo`, file in dwftn's repo at `examples/hello.lgo`). Their brief points them at the dwxtc Trunk+Yew template. No dcftn dependency in their critical path beyond the .lgo.

**No new sagas for dcftn.** And my two flagged issues from the previous turn — the PATH-installed `fortran` wrapper looking for `hello.s` in the wrong place, and `hello-fortran.lgo` not actually being at `lib/cor24/` despite being claimed there — aren't addressed in any of these updates. If you want me to ship the wrapper-fix as `pr/fortran-wrapper-fix` (recommend the heredoc approach for self-containment), say the word.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# fortran -- compile an FTI-0 .f source to COR24 assembly.
#
# Usage:
#   fortran <input.f>            # writes COR24 assembly to stdout
#   fortran --help
#
# CURRENT STATUS: Path A only.
#
# This wrapper is a Path-A fixture per
#   tools/briefs/dcftn-fortran-hello-world.md
# It short-circuits for the canonical Hello-World source
#   examples/hello.f
# by emitting the pre-baked
#   examples/hello.s
# on stdout. Any other input errors out -- the full FTI-0 compiler
# (the SNOBOL4-based pipeline that consumes arbitrary .f sources)
# is a separate parallel effort tracked under the milestone-1
# saga (currently archived behind the dcemu --lgo + --uart-file
# bug; see docs/snobol4-blockers.md).
#
# When the full compiler is ready, this wrapper will grow a Path B
# branch that invokes
#   cor24-emu --lgo $TOOLROOT/../lib/cor24/snobol4.lgo \
#             --uart-file <our compiler.sno> \
#             -u "$(cat <input.f>)"<EOT>
# instead.
#
# End-to-end pipeline (Path A):
#
#   scripts/fortran examples/hello.f > /tmp/hello.s
#   cor24-asm /tmp/hello.s -o /tmp/hello.lgo
#   cor24-emu --lgo /tmp/hello.lgo --quiet --speed 0 -n 1000000
#       => prints "Hello, World!\n", exit 0

set -euo pipefail

SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "$0")" && pwd)"
REPO_DIR="$(cd "$SCRIPT_DIR/.." && pwd)"

if [ "${1:-}" = "--help" ] || [ "${1:-}" = "-h" ]; then
    sed -n '2,/^$/p' "$0" | sed 's/^# \?//'
    exit 0
fi

if [ $# -lt 1 ]; then
    echo "fortran: error: expected one argument (an .f source file)" >&2
    echo "Usage: fortran <input.f>" >&2
    exit 2
fi

INPUT_F="$1"
if [ ! -f "$INPUT_F" ]; then
    echo "fortran: error: $INPUT_F not found." >&2
    exit 1
fi

INPUT_BASENAME="$(basename "$INPUT_F")"
HELLO_S="$REPO_DIR/examples/hello.s"

# Path A short-circuit: hello.f -> pre-baked hello.s.
if [ "$INPUT_BASENAME" = "hello.f" ]; then
    if [ ! -f "$HELLO_S" ]; then
        echo "fortran: error: $HELLO_S missing -- repo state inconsistent." >&2
        exit 1
    fi
    cat "$HELLO_S"
    exit 0
fi

# Path B is not implemented yet.
cat >&2 <<EOF
fortran: error: only hello.f is supported in the current Path-A
short-circuit. Input '$INPUT_BASENAME' would require the full FTI-0
compiler, which is a separate parallel effort.

  See: docs/snobol4-blockers.md
       tools/briefs/dcftn-fortran-hello-world.md
EOF
exit 1
